title: CHMAS: A Coupled Hierarchical Framework for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Presented at the 2026 American Control Conference (ACC 2026). This version corrects the author names in Reference 1. The technical content, results, and conclusions are unchanged
summary:
Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) systems face fundamental
  challenges in balancing global coordination with local execution
  across different temporal scales. This paper introduces the Coupled
  Hierarchical Multi-Agent System (CHMAS), a novel framework that
  decomposes multi-agent decision-making into centralized strategic
  planning and distributed tactical execution with bidirectional
  information flow. The strategic layer integrates all agents' states
  with an exclusive global environmental state to generate guidance
  actions every $T$ timesteps, while tactical agents execute
  distributed policies augmented by strategic guidance and local
  neighborhood observations. Unlike existing hierarchical approaches
  with unidirectional control, CHMAS establishes a feedback mechanism
  where accumulated tactical rewards influence strategic objectives
  through a coupling coefficient $λ$, ensuring strategic plans
  remain grounded in tactical feasibility. To address the
  non-stationarity inherent in hierarchical learning, we propose an
  asynchronous update protocol where strategic parameters update every
  $N_f$ tactical episodes, allowing tactical policies to converge to
  quasi-stationary points between strategic changes. We present both a
  general bi-level formulation capturing full system dynamics and a
  tractable additive approximation enabling rigorous analysis.
  Theoretical analysis proves that this asynchronous scheme achieves
  $\mathcal{O}(\log K/\sqrt{K})$ convergence for the strategic layer
  after $K$ strategic updates under standard assumptions. Experimental
  validation in a multi-agent foraging domain demonstrates successful
  learning of spatially partitioned exploration strategies, with both
  layers converging stably despite hierarchical coupling.

AI.tldr: CHMAS introduces a novel hierarchical MARL framework with bidirectional coordination between strategic planning and tactical execution, featuring asynchronous updates to handle non-stationarity and achieving provable convergence.
AI.motivation: MARL systems struggle to balance global coordination with local execution across different temporal scales. Existing hierarchical approaches typically use unidirectional control, lacking feedback mechanisms to ensure strategic plans remain grounded in tactical feasibility.
AI.method: The Coupled Hierarchical Multi-Agent System (CHMAS) decomposes decision-making into centralized strategic planning (using all agents' states plus global environmental state) and distributed tactical execution (using strategic guidance and local observations). It establishes bidirectional information flow with tactical rewards influencing strategic objectives via coupling coefficient λ. To handle non-stationarity, an asynchronous update protocol updates strategic parameters every N_f tactical episodes, allowing tactical policies to converge between strategic changes.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis proves the asynchronous scheme achieves O(log K/√K) convergence for the strategic layer after K strategic updates. Experimental validation in multi-agent foraging demonstrates successful learning of spatially partitioned exploration strategies, with both layers converging stably despite hierarchical coupling.
AI.conclusion: CHMAS provides an effective framework for hierarchical MARL with bidirectional coordination, addressing fundamental challenges in balancing global coordination with local execution across temporal scales while maintaining theoretical guarantees and practical stability.

title: Same Game, Different Story: A Minimal Conservative Strategic Robustness Benchmark for Large Language Model Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly operate in strategic settings where outcomes depend on the actions of other agents. This raises a reliability question: will a model choose consistently when the same incentives are presented through different narratives? We introduce Same Game, Different Story, a benchmark that defines strategic robustness as invariance of model-induced action distributions under payoff-preserving changes in framing. We illustrate the framework through a secondary analysis of published aggregate cooperation rates for GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and LLaMa-2 across four social-dilemma games. The retained comparison covers business and friend-sharing framings, representing 24 model-game-context cells and 7,200 decisions in the source study. Because trial-level data were unavailable, approximate counts were reconstructed from published figures; the resulting estimates are therefore illustrative rather than an exact replication. Under the paper's conservative transformation, pooled strategic robustness is 0.783, and friend-sharing framing increases cooperation by 0.307 relative to business framing. The results indicate that social-relational framing can substantially alter LLM behavior even when the underlying action sets and payoffs remain fixed. Strategic robustness should therefore be evaluated separately from strategic competence, using families of payoff-equivalent prompts rather than a single presentation of a game.

AI.tldr: A benchmark called "Same Game, Different Story" tests LLM strategic robustness by measuring if models make consistent decisions when payoffs stay the same but framing narratives change, finding social-relational framing significantly affects behavior.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents increasingly operate in strategic settings where outcomes depend on other agents' actions, there's a reliability question about whether models choose consistently when the same incentives are presented through different narratives. The paper investigates how framing affects LLM decision-making even when underlying payoffs remain identical.
AI.method: Introduces "Same Game, Different Story" benchmark that defines strategic robustness as invariance of model-induced action distributions under payoff-preserving changes in framing. Conducts secondary analysis of published aggregate cooperation rates for GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and LLaMa-2 across four social-dilemma games. Compares business vs friend-sharing framings (24 model-game-context cells, 7,200 decisions), with approximate counts reconstructed from published figures when trial-level data was unavailable.
AI.result: Pooled strategic robustness is 0.783, and friend-sharing framing increases cooperation by 0.307 relative to business framing. Results show social-relational framing substantially alters LLM behavior even when action sets and payoffs remain fixed.
AI.conclusion: Strategic robustness should be evaluated separately from strategic competence, using families of payoff-equivalent prompts rather than a single presentation of a game. Framing matters significantly for LLM decision-making in strategic contexts, highlighting the need to test models across different narrative presentations of the same underlying incentives.

title: Not Birds of a Feather: Personality-Based Partner Selection in LLM Agents
summary: Multi-agent LLM systems increasingly let one agent choose which other agents to work with, and agents are increasingly given personalities through personas. We test whether Big Five personality alone influences partner selection when capability is explicitly held constant. Host agents chose among six validated candidate archetypes -- five marked high on one trait (openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism) plus a balanced control -- presented with randomized names and ordering across five task categories (375 trials). With neutral hosts (Study 1, n=150), selection departed drastically from chance ($χ^2(5)=325.8$, $p<.001$), following a task-stereotype map: the open archetype won 100% of creative trials, the conscientious archetype 90-97% of strategic, synthesis, and problem-solving trials, and the neurotic archetype 37% of analytical trials (Cramer's V=.74); the extraverted, agreeable, and balanced archetypes were almost never chosen, although human meta-analyses identify team agreeableness as among the strongest personality predictors of team performance. With personality-assigned hosts (Study 2, n=225), and contrary to human similarity-attraction, self-similar partners were selected below chance (11.1% vs. 16.7%, p=.025) and at greater-than-chance trait distance (p<.0001); conscientious hosts diversified away from their own archetype, recruiting vigilant and open partners. Personality-based selection in LLM agents is real, strong, task-stereotyped, non-homophilous, and miscalibrated against human team-performance evidence -- with direct implications for bias auditing in agent marketplaces.

AI.tldr: LLM agents select partners based on personality stereotypes that match task types, but these selections don't follow human similarity attraction patterns and contradict actual team performance evidence.
AI.motivation: As multi-agent LLM systems allow agents to choose partners and assign them personalities, there's a need to understand whether personality alone influences partner selection when capability is held constant, and whether this aligns with human psychological patterns and performance evidence.
AI.method: Two studies: Study 1 had neutral host agents choose among six validated Big Five personality archetypes across five task categories (375 trials). Study 2 assigned personality to hosts and examined selection patterns against human similarity-attraction tendencies.
AI.result: Selection departed drastically from chance, following task-stereotype mapping: creative tasks → open archetype (100%), strategic/synthesis/problem-solving → conscientious (90-97%), analytical → neurotic (37%). Contrary to human similarity-attraction, self-similar partners were selected below chance, with conscientious hosts diversifying away from their own archetype.
AI.conclusion: Personality-based selection in LLM agents is real, strong, task-stereotyped, non-homophilous, and miscalibrated against human team-performance evidence, highlighting bias auditing implications for agent marketplaces.

title: Dreamer-CPC: Message Learning with World Models for Decentralized Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures. Under review at ICONIP 2026
summary: In multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), inter-agent communication is effective for improving performance under partial observability. Representation learning-based approaches enable decentralized agents to learn messages grounded in their own observations, but they rely only on current observations and cannot convey information accumulated over time. We propose Dreamer-CPC, a decentralized model-based MARL method that integrates message learning based on Collective Predictive Coding (CPC) into the world model of DreamerV3. Each agent independently maintains a world model and a message module, and infers and exchanges messages from the latent states of the world model that reflect the history of past observations and actions. We evaluated Dreamer-CPC in two environments: Observer, a non-cooperative information-sharing task, and CatchApple, a newly introduced task in which task-relevant observations are temporarily missing. In both environments, Dreamer-CPC outperformed IPPO-CPC, an existing CPC-based method that generates messages from current observations, as well as no-communication baselines. In particular, in CatchApple, Dreamer-CPC achieved 4 to 5 times the episode return of IPPO-CPC, demonstrating effective coordination where other methods fail due to missing observations. These results suggest that communication grounded in the latent dynamics of world models can support decentralized decision-making when current observations alone are insufficient.

AI.tldr: Dreamer-CPC is a decentralized model-based MARL method that integrates message learning based on Collective Predictive Coding into DreamerV3's world model, enabling agents to communicate using latent states that accumulate historical information rather than just current observations.
AI.motivation: In MARL under partial observability, existing representation learning approaches for communication only use current observations and cannot convey accumulated historical information, limiting coordination when observations are incomplete or missing.
AI.method: Proposes Dreamer-CPC where each agent independently maintains a world model and message module, infers messages from latent states of the world model (which reflect history of past observations and actions), and exchanges these messages for decentralized coordination.
AI.result: Outperformed IPPO-CPC and no-communication baselines in two environments: Observer (non-cooperative information-sharing) and CatchApple (new task with temporarily missing observations). Achieved 4-5 times higher episode return than IPPO-CPC in CatchApple where observations are missing.
AI.conclusion: Communication grounded in the latent dynamics of world models can effectively support decentralized decision-making when current observations alone are insufficient, enabling coordination that other methods fail at.

title: FineServe: A Fine-Grained Dataset and Characterization of Global LLM Serving Workloads
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as always-on online services, making efficient LLM serving a critical systems challenge. Achieving low latency and high throughput under volatile demand requires deep understanding of real-world serving workloads, yet existing studies often rely on proxy traces or coarse-grained characterizations that fail to capture the heterogeneity of modern multi-model LLM platforms. We present FineServe, an in-the-wild, multi-model LLM serving workload dataset collected from a global commercial marketplace, enabling fine-grained characterization of real-world serving dynamics across heterogeneous models and tasks. Leveraging FineServe, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of arrival dynamics and token behavior, revealing fundamentally different fluctuation regimes across model architectures, scales and task intents. Building on these insights, we develop the FineServe workload generator, which composes fine-grained model-aware workloads into configurable mixtures tailored for benchmarking multi-model serving platforms. By exposing these fine-grained workload dynamics, FineServe provides a realistic foundation for evaluating routing, scheduling, and capacity-planning strategies in LLM serving systems. FineServe is available at https://github.com/hihiztc1/FineServe.

AI.tldr: FineServe is a real-world LLM serving workload dataset collected from a global commercial marketplace that enables fine-grained characterization of serving dynamics across heterogeneous models and tasks, plus a workload generator for benchmarking multi-model serving platforms.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as always-on online services, making efficient serving a critical systems challenge. Existing studies often rely on proxy traces or coarse-grained characterizations that fail to capture the heterogeneity of modern multi-model LLM platforms.
AI.method: Collected an in-the-wild, multi-model LLM serving workload dataset (FineServe) from a global commercial marketplace. Conducted comprehensive analysis of arrival dynamics and token behavior. Developed the FineServe workload generator that composes fine-grained model-aware workloads into configurable mixtures.
AI.result: Revealed fundamentally different fluctuation regimes across model architectures, scales and task intents. Exposed fine-grained workload dynamics that provide a realistic foundation for evaluating routing, scheduling, and capacity-planning strategies in LLM serving systems.
AI.conclusion: FineServe provides a realistic foundation for evaluating multi-model LLM serving systems and addressing the critical challenge of efficient LLM serving under volatile demand, with the dataset publicly available on GitHub.

title: Native Multi-Dimensional Subquadratic Operators via Input Dependent Long Convolutions
summary: Subquadratic alternatives to attention require compromises when applied to multi-dimensional data: standard convolutions lack global receptive fields and input dependency, while recurrent models require rasterizing data such as images, volumes, and partial differential equation (PDE) into an ad-hoc $1\rm D$ scan order that violates their spatial structure. We introduce \textit{HyenaND}, a subquadratic, global, input-dependent operator that acts directly on the native geometry of multidimensional data through convolutions with implicitly parametrized global, input-dependent multi-dimensional convolutional kernels. Our CUDA implementation, \texttt{nSubQ}, fuses the FFT-convolution path to turn HyenaND's $\mathcal{O}(L \log L)$ scaling into wall-clock speedups. Across long-context genomics, computer vision, medical imaging, and PDE modeling, pure HyenaND stacks match the accuracy of strong attention baselines, while hybrid configurations that interleave HyenaND and attention layers outperform both pure attention and strong recurrence-based hybrids.

AI.tldr: HyenaND is a subquadratic operator for multidimensional data that uses global, input-dependent convolutions with implicitly parametrized kernels, achieving attention-level accuracy without quadratic scaling.
AI.motivation: Existing subquadratic alternatives to attention have limitations for multidimensional data: standard convolutions lack global receptive fields and input dependency, while recurrent models require rasterizing data into 1D scans that violate spatial structure. There's a need for efficient operators that preserve multidimensional geometry while maintaining global dependencies.
AI.method: HyenaND uses convolutions with implicitly parametrized global, input-dependent multidimensional convolutional kernels that act directly on native data geometry. Implemented with CUDA (nSubQ) using FFT-convolution fusion to achieve O(L log L) scaling with actual wall-clock speedups.
AI.result: Pure HyenaND stacks match attention accuracy across genomics, computer vision, medical imaging, and PDE modeling. Hybrid configurations (HyenaND + attention layers) outperform both pure attention and recurrence-based hybrids.
AI.conclusion: HyenaND provides an effective subquadratic alternative to attention for multidimensional data, addressing limitations of existing methods while maintaining accuracy and efficiency, with hybrid architectures offering superior performance.

title: Harnessing Disagreement: Detecting Correlated Agreement Blindness in Multi-Agent Triage
comment: 14 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at PAAMS 2026; this is the author's pre-review submitted version
summary: Disagreement-triggered escalation can create a structural blind spot in multi-agent arbitration: as base learners improve, they tend to converge, weakening safety monitoring where correlated failures concentrate. We term this correlated agreement blindness and present ARAT (Arbitrated Reasoning Agents for Alarm Triage), a directed-star system combining an inductive Random Forest (RF) agent, an analogical case-based k-nearest neighbour (k-NN) agent, and a calibrated meta-model to mitigate this effect. On 82,332 holdout samples from the UNSW-NB15 network intrusion detection dataset, 57.2% of errors occur under agreement and 90.6% of dangerous under-predictions evade disagreement-based monitoring even after conservative override; ablation shows that strengthening base learners increases error correlation while reducing disagreement. ARAT reduces under-prediction relative to soft voting from 4.80% to 1.70% via conservative override (-2.6pp) and a safety-flag gate (-0.5pp), demonstrating architectural gains. Cross-dataset validation on clinical readmission supports these indicators, suggesting that diversification improves safety only when it generates productive disagreement rather than convergence. These results indicate that disagreement-triggered escalation can be blind to correlated failure, a risk that may intensify as agentic pipelines deploy increasingly capable, correlated models.

AI.tldr: ARAT is a multi-agent arbitration system designed to address correlated agreement blindness - where improved base learners converge and create safety monitoring blind spots - using RF and k-NN agents with calibrated meta-modeling to reduce under-prediction errors.
AI.motivation: Disagreement-triggered escalation systems have a structural blind spot: as base learners improve, they tend to converge, weakening safety monitoring where correlated failures concentrate. This "correlated agreement blindness" means errors can occur under agreement and dangerous under-predictions can evade disagreement-based monitoring, creating safety risks in multi-agent systems.
AI.method: ARAT (Arbitrated Reasoning Agents for Alarm Triage) uses a directed-star system combining an inductive Random Forest (RF) agent and an analogical case-based k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) agent, with a calibrated meta-model to mitigate correlated agreement blindness. The system includes conservative override and safety-flag gating mechanisms, validated on 82,332 holdout samples from UNSW-NB15 network intrusion detection dataset, with cross-dataset validation on clinical readmission data.
AI.result: ARAT reduces under-prediction from 4.80% to 1.70% (-2.6pp from conservative override and -0.5pp from safety-flag gate). Analysis shows 57.2% of errors occur under agreement, and 90.6% of dangerous under-predictions evade disagreement-based monitoring even after conservative override. Ablation studies reveal that strengthening base learners increases error correlation while reducing disagreement, validating the correlated agreement blindness hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: Disagreement-triggered escalation can be blind to correlated failure, a risk that intensifies as agentic pipelines deploy increasingly capable, correlated models. Diversification improves safety only when it generates productive disagreement rather than convergence. ARAT demonstrates architectural gains in addressing correlated agreement blindness through its multi-agent arbitration approach.

title: Hybrid LSTM-Graph Neural Framework for Robust Financial Fraud Detection and Adversarial Resilience
summary: Financial institutions face significant challenges in detecting sophisticated money laundering patterns, such as smurfing and layering, due to extreme data imbalance (0.13% fraud rate) and evolving adversarial evasion tactics. This paper proposes FraudShield AI, a hybrid framework that integrates Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks with hand-crafted Graph Topological Features to capture both temporal sequences and structural relational context. By engineering network-centric features including PageRank Centrality, In-Degree dynamics, and a custom Flow Ratio, the system shifts the detection paradigm from isolated transaction analysis to network-level forensics. A Focal Loss objective is used to address class imbalance, and a dynamic thresholding mechanism is introduced to improve resilience against low-value smurfing attacks. Experimental evaluation on the PaySim dataset shows that the proposed hybrid model substantially outperforms Logistic Regression and XGBoost baselines in Precision, Recall, and F1-Score, particularly on hard-to-detect micro-transaction fraud patterns. An ablation study confirms the complementary contribution of both the temporal and topological components.

AI.tldr: FraudShield AI: A hybrid LSTM-graph framework for detecting money laundering patterns like smurfing and layering, combining temporal sequence analysis with graph topological features to overcome data imbalance and evolving evasion tactics.
AI.motivation: Financial institutions struggle with detecting sophisticated money laundering patterns (smurfing, layering) due to extreme data imbalance (0.13% fraud rate) and evolving adversarial evasion tactics. Current approaches often fail to capture both temporal patterns and structural relationships in financial networks.
AI.method: Proposed FraudShield AI - a hybrid framework integrating LSTM networks with hand-crafted Graph Topological Features. Uses network-centric features including PageRank Centrality, In-Degree dynamics, and custom Flow Ratio. Employs Focal Loss objective to address class imbalance and dynamic thresholding mechanism for resilience against low-value smurfing attacks.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on PaySim dataset shows the hybrid model substantially outperforms Logistic Regression and XGBoost baselines in Precision, Recall, and F1-Score, particularly on hard-to-detect micro-transaction fraud patterns. Ablation study confirms complementary contributions of both temporal and topological components.
AI.conclusion: FraudShield AI effectively shifts detection paradigm from isolated transaction analysis to network-level forensics, successfully addressing both temporal and structural aspects of money laundering while handling data imbalance and adversarial evasion tactics.

title: Bayesian Wind Tunnels for Model Selection
summary:
Prior work has shown that transformers can perform exact Bayesian filtering within a fixed
  hypothesis class. Can they also perform Bayesian model selection -- identifying the correct
  hypothesis class from data? We introduce model-selection Bayesian wind tunnels: controlled
  environments where ground-truth posteriors over hypothesis classes are available in closed
  form. Using fixed-point-free involutions -- whose defining property f(f(x))=x is purely
  relational -- a 2.8M-parameter transformer achieves 0.01-bit entropy agreement with the
  Bayesian optimum (3 seeds), with both integer tokens and opaque symbols whose meanings
  change every episode. This extends to non-nested comparisons: involutions vs. 3-cycles
  (where neither class is a subset of the other) achieve class-posterior MAE under 0.001,
  demonstrating genuine model selection beyond simplicity/subset bias. We then identify a
  sharp perceptual access condition: when the discriminative statistic requires arithmetic --
  modular addition (rotations) or multiplication (f(x)=cx mod p) -- model selection succeeds
  with integer tokens but fails completely with opaque symbols, and this boundary persists
  under 112x scaling (2.8M to 316M parameters). A stationarity control confirms the operative
  factor: opaque tokens with a fixed relabeling succeed (0.009-bit MAE), showing that stable
  semantics, not integer identity, enable circuit compilation. Header subtask diagnostics
  localize the failure to the composition of header inversion with arithmetic rather than
  header parsing itself. Probing frontier LLMs on the same tasks shows qualitative Bayesian
  behavior but a large calibration gap (~55x), measured through lossy probes and therefore
  directional rather than exact.

AI.tldr: Transformers can perform Bayesian model selection for purely relational involution classes but fail when discriminative statistics require arithmetic with opaque symbols, unless symbols have stable semantics.
AI.motivation: To determine if transformers can perform Bayesian model selection (identifying correct hypothesis classes from data) beyond just Bayesian filtering within fixed classes, and to investigate conditions under which this is possible.
AI.method: Create model-selection Bayesian wind tunnels with ground-truth posteriors over hypothesis classes. Use fixed-point-free involutions (f(f(x))=x) and 3-cycles for non-nested comparisons. Test transformers (2.8M to 316M parameters) with integer tokens vs. opaque symbols. Analyze perceptual access conditions through arithmetic tasks (modular addition/multiplication) and stationarity controls with fixed relabeling.
AI.result: Transformers achieve 0.01-bit entropy agreement with Bayesian optimum for involution model selection with both integer tokens and opaque symbols. For non-nested comparisons (involutions vs 3-cycles), achieve class-posterior MAE under 0.001. However, model selection fails with opaque symbols when discriminative statistics require arithmetic (modular operations), but succeeds with stable semantics through fixed relabeling (0.009-bit MAE). Larger models (316M) don't overcome this boundary. Frontier LLMs show qualitative Bayesian behavior but large calibration gap (~55x).
AI.conclusion: Transformers can perform genuine Bayesian model selection for purely relational hypothesis classes, but success depends on perceptual access to stable semantics rather than specific token identities. Arithmetic composition with opaque symbols presents a boundary that stable semantics can overcome. Practical Bayesian model selection in transformers requires circuits that can compile from stable semantic representations.

title: Knowledge-Centric Self-Improvement
summary: Self-improving AI systems typically treat the agent as the object that improves, by optimizing prompts, workflows, harnesses, or even the agent's own code. This agent-centric view can make improvements expensive to maintain and difficult to transfer, because gains become tied to a particular agent design, task distribution, or adaptation run. We study a complementary paradigm: knowledge-centric self-improvement, in which agents remain generic and disposable while the persistent object is a curated knowledge base that agents can leverage for future tasks. We conduct controlled case studies to operationalize this idea via a simple protocol. Agents attempt one task, then contribute evidence-grounded insights to a shared knowledge base via task-level and cross-task forums, followed by knowledge distillation. Because self-improvement is contained in the knowledge rather than the agent, improvement can be more inspectable, transferable, and portable. Across abstract reasoning, coding, and terminal benchmarks, this protocol improves solve rates while reducing dollar cost relative to agent-centric baselines. The resulting distilled knowledge also transfers to held-out tasks and across LLM families, indicating that the improvement is not merely an LLM- or run-specific behavior. These results support a new view of self-improving agentic systems: progress can be driven primarily by the curated persistent knowledge. Code is available at https://github.com/recursive-knowledge/KSI.

AI.tldr: Self-improving AI systems should focus on building curated knowledge bases rather than improving individual agents - this knowledge-centric approach makes improvements more transferable, inspectable, and cost-effective across various reasoning and coding tasks.
AI.motivation: Current agent-centric self-improvement approaches tie improvements to specific agent designs, task distributions, or adaptation runs, making gains expensive to maintain and difficult to transfer. The authors propose studying knowledge-centric self-improvement as a complementary paradigm.
AI.method: Simple protocol where agents attempt tasks, then contribute evidence-grounded insights to shared knowledge bases via task-level and cross-task forums, followed by knowledge distillation. Agents remain generic and disposable while knowledge becomes the persistent, curated object.
AI.result: Across abstract reasoning, coding, and terminal benchmarks, the protocol improves solve rates while reducing dollar cost relative to agent-centric baselines. The distilled knowledge transfers to held-out tasks and across LLM families, showing improvement is not LLM- or run-specific.
AI.conclusion: Progress in self-improving agentic systems can be driven primarily by curated persistent knowledge rather than agent improvements. Knowledge-centric self-improvement enables more inspectable, transferable, and portable improvements across different systems and tasks.

title: OpenEvoShield: Dual Non-Stationary Continual Defense for Open-World Multi-Agent System Attacks
summary: LLM-based multi-agent systems (LLM-MAS) are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, where adversaries inject malicious instructions through inter-agent communication to propagate harmful behaviors. Unlike static threats, these attacks are doubly dynamic: adversaries refine injection strategies against deployed defenses while normal-agent behavior drifts with system expansion. Existing defenses treat deployment as a closed-world problem and degrade rapidly once either distribution shifts beyond training coverage. We propose OpenEvoShield, a co-evolutionary continual defense framework for LLM-MAS. An asymmetric rate controller (M1) decouples fast attack-side and slow normal-side learning rates from dual drift signals. A normal-boundary updater (M2) maintains a dynamic behavioral boundary at the slow rate, while an EWC-regularized policy ensemble (M3) fast-adapts without catastrophic forgetting. An energy-based multi-granularity detector (M4) fuses node-, subgraph-, and graph-level evidence to classify novel attacks as out-of-distribution. Experiments over 100 deployment rounds across five benchmarks and four MAS topologies show that OpenEvoShield outperforms static and continual baselines, detecting most previously unseen attacks while keeping false positive rates low.

AI.tldr: OpenEvoShield is a co-evolutionary continual defense framework that protects LLM-based multi-agent systems from dynamic adversarial attacks through asymmetric learning rates, behavioral boundary maintenance, policy adaptation, and multi-granularity detection.
AI.motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly used in safety-critical applications where adversaries inject malicious instructions through inter-agent communication. Existing defenses treat deployment as a closed-world problem and degrade rapidly when attack strategies evolve or normal-agent behavior drifts with system expansion.
AI.method: OpenEvoShield uses four key components: 1) Asymmetric rate controller decouples fast attack-side and slow normal-side learning rates; 2) Normal-boundary updater maintains dynamic behavioral boundaries; 3) EWC-regularized policy ensemble fast-adapts without catastrophic forgetting; 4) Energy-based multi-granularity detector fuses node-, subgraph-, and graph-level evidence to classify novel attacks.
AI.result: Experiments over 100 deployment rounds across five benchmarks and four MAS topologies show OpenEvoShield outperforms static and continual baselines, detecting most previously unseen attacks while keeping false positive rates low.
AI.conclusion: OpenEvoShield effectively addresses the doubly dynamic threat landscape in LLM-MAS by maintaining adaptability to evolving attacks while preserving stability against normal behavioral drift, making it suitable for real-world safety-critical deployments.

title: CruiseBench: A Real-Flight-Aligned N-CMAPSS Benchmark for Engine RUL Prediction
summary: Remaining useful life (RUL) prediction estimates how long an engine can continue safe operation and is central to maintenance planning. N-CMAPSS extends C-MAPSS by simulating run-to-failure aero-engine trajectories using recorded real-flight profiles and retaining complete within-flight time series rather than cycle-level snapshots. However, this added realism reduces evaluation control because full-flight records increase data volume and entangle degradation cues with operating-regime variation, complicating preprocessing choices and direct comparisons of RUL modeling performance. To mitigate this issue, this paper proposes CruiseBench, a cruise-stage RUL benchmark derived from N-CMAPSS. It introduces CPM-N-CMAPSS (Cruising-Period Mask for N-CMAPSS), a mask artifact that stores cycle-local cruising intervals identified by the common-altitude method for the nine accessible subdatasets. CruiseBench applies a fixed protocol to the masked rows, using scenario descriptors and measured sensors as inputs while excluding virtual sensors, health parameters, and auxiliary metadata from the feature tensor, preserving native-resolution windows, and applying dataset-wise RUL caps. Experiments with LSTM, GRU, TCN, and TSMixer provide baseline results for this setting. Under CruiseBench-eta5-W256-S10, TSMixer obtains the lowest average RMSE, $3.4\pm1.71$, and Saxena score, $(2.50\pm2.99)\times 10^{4}$. Ablation studies show that flight-stage selection, temporal downscaling method, and RUL-cap threshold affect reported results. With its fixed cruise-stage protocol, CruiseBench provides a reproducible sub-benchmark for controlled RUL model comparison and CPM-N-CMAPSS provides a stage-specific data foundation for future transfer-learning and domain-adaptation studies.

AI.tldr: CruiseBench creates a reproducible benchmark for RUL prediction by extracting only cruise-stage data from N-CMAPSS using CPM-N-CMAPSS masks, enabling controlled model comparison with standardized preprocessing.
AI.motivation: N-CMAPSS provides realistic engine failure data but makes evaluation difficult due to high data volume and entanglement of degradation signals with operating regime variations, complicating preprocessing and fair model comparisons.
AI.method: Proposes CruiseBench using CPM-N-CMAPSS masks to identify cruise-stage intervals via common-altitude method, applies fixed protocol using only scenario descriptors and measured sensors, excludes virtual sensors/health parameters, preserves native resolution, and applies dataset-wise RUL caps.
AI.result: TSMixer achieves best performance with average RMSE of 3.4±1.71 and Saxena score of (2.50±2.99)×10⁴ under CruiseBench-eta5-W256-S10 configuration; ablation studies show flight-stage selection, downscaling method, and RUL-cap threshold significantly affect results.
AI.conclusion: CruiseBench provides reproducible sub-benchmark for controlled RUL model comparison, while CPM-N-CMAPSS offers stage-specific foundation for future transfer learning and domain adaptation studies in engine health monitoring.

title: Autonomous Collaborative Learning Among an Ensemble of Tsetlin Machines with Consensus-Based Inference
summary: Tsetlin Machine (TM) is a rule-based machine-learning algorithm comprising collectives of two-action Tsetlin Automata (TAs) that cooperatively form conjunctive logical clauses from Boolean inputs through stochastic feedback. Although few recent studies have examined TM Federated Learning, the broader area of distributed and decentralized TM learning has not received much attention in the existing literature and warrants further exploration. In this work, we propose a paradigm for decentralized collaborative learning under a vertical feature-partitioning setting among an ensemble of Tsetlin Machines using consensus-based inference. Within this decentralized paradigm, each agent maintains its own private TM model, and there is no exchange of raw data among agents. Inference combines individual agents model predictions into a global consensus. The paradigm accommodates heterogeneous TM-based agents with differing data acquisition means, local data distributions, or computational resources, thereby facilitating the integration and fusion of information in settings such as multi-modal sensing environments. Experiments conducted using two-dimensional grid and connected graph network topologies demonstrate that the classification accuracies achieved are comparable to those of centralized models.

AI.tldr: A decentralized collaborative learning paradigm for Tsetlin Machines using consensus-based inference under vertical feature partitioning, achieving comparable accuracy to centralized models without sharing raw data.
AI.motivation: Distributed and decentralized Tsetlin Machine learning has received limited attention in existing literature, particularly for collaborative learning scenarios where agents have private data and heterogeneous characteristics. The authors seek to address this gap by exploring decentralized TM learning that doesn't require raw data exchange.
AI.method: Proposes a decentralized paradigm with vertical feature partitioning where each agent maintains its own private TM model. Uses consensus-based inference to combine individual agent predictions into global consensus without exchanging raw data. Supports heterogeneous agents with varying data acquisition methods, local distributions, and computational resources.
AI.result: Experiments using two-dimensional grid and connected graph network topologies demonstrate that the classification accuracies achieved are comparable to those of centralized models, validating the effectiveness of the decentralized approach.
AI.conclusion: The proposed decentralized collaborative learning paradigm for Tsetlin Machines is effective for settings with heterogeneous agents and private data, achieving performance comparable to centralized approaches while maintaining data privacy and accommodating agent diversity.

title: Benchmarking Confidential GPU Inference on NVIDIA H100 under Intel TDX
summary: Confidential computing is becoming a practical deployment requirement for AI inference workloads that process sensitive inputs or protect proprietary model assets. However, the performance cost of enabling confidential execution for GPU-accelerated large language model serving remains workload dependent and operationally important. This paper presents a benchmark study comparing standard non-confidential execution with confidential computing mode on a single NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU hosted in an Intel TDX confidential instance. The evaluation uses two representative language models, Mistral-7B v0.1 and Qwen3-30B-A3B, and measures time to first token, end-to-end request latency, per-request token generation throughput, global token throughput, and closed-loop request throughput under increasing concurrency. In fixed request-rate experiments, confidential mode increases average TTFT by 21.8% for Mistral-7B and 27.8% for Qwen3-30B-A3B, while global token throughput drops by 17.7% and 21.1%, respectively. In closed-loop concurrency experiments, throughput gaps remain in the 11.5-20.2% range, but the larger model reaches its saturation knee earlier under confidential mode. The results suggest that confidential GPU inference can retain usable throughput under load, but capacity planning must account for both the steady throughput penalty and the earlier saturation behavior observed for larger models.

AI.tldr: Benchmark study shows confidential computing on NVIDIA H100 GPU causes 17-27% performance penalty for LLM inference, with larger models reaching saturation earlier under confidential mode.
AI.motivation: Confidential computing is increasingly required for AI inference workloads dealing with sensitive data or proprietary models, but the performance impact of enabling confidential execution for GPU-accelerated LLM serving is not well understood and operationally important for deployment decisions.
AI.method: Benchmark study comparing standard non-confidential execution with confidential computing mode on NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU in Intel TDX confidential instance. Evaluated two language models (Mistral-7B v0.1 and Qwen3-30B-A3B) measuring multiple performance metrics: time to first token, end-to-end latency, per-request token generation throughput, global token throughput, and closed-loop request throughput under increasing concurrency.
AI.result: Confidential mode increased average TTFT by 21.8% for Mistral-7B and 27.8% for Qwen3-30B; global token throughput dropped by 17.7% and 21.1% respectively. Throughput gaps remained 11.5-20.2% in closed-loop experiments, with larger models reaching saturation earlier under confidential mode.
AI.conclusion: Confidential GPU inference maintains usable throughput under load, but capacity planning must account for both steady throughput penalties (17-27%) and earlier saturation behavior observed for larger models in confidential computing environments.

title: Air Quality Arena: A Large-Scale Multi-Region Ground Monitoring Dataset and Benchmark for Air Quality Forecasting with Time-Series Foundation Models
summary: Air pollution causes an estimated 7.9 million premature deaths annually, making accurate forecasting a critical public health priority. Machine learning is increasingly being applied to forecast air pollution levels, yet existing benchmarks remain narrow in both geographic scope and pollutant coverage, and fail to evaluate the latest generation of time series foundation models (TSFMs) on real world, large scale data. We present Air Quality Arena (AQA), a large scale multi-country and multi-pollutant dataset (AQA-Data) and benchmark (AQA-Bench) to address this gap. AQA covers 6 major pollutants over a three year period across 7 diverse countries and 4 continents, with more than 14,000 station-pollutant series, aiming to provide a comprehensive benchmark for air quality tasks. We benchmark this dataset across 11 leading time series foundation models and classical baselines to assess performance on short-term air quality forecasting. Our results demonstrate that TSFMs are effective zero-shot forecasters and consistently outperform classical baselines, with our top-performing model employing a cross-modal architecture that leverages a vision foundation model for time series forecasting. AQA is publicly released at AirQualityArena.github.io

AI.tldr: AQA provides the first large-scale multi-country, multi-pollutant air quality dataset and benchmark, demonstrating that time series foundation models (TSFMs) outperform classical methods for zero-shot air quality forecasting.
AI.motivation: Air pollution causes 7.9 million premature deaths annually, making accurate forecasting critical for public health. Existing benchmarks are narrow in geographic scope and pollutant coverage, and fail to evaluate the latest time series foundation models on real-world large-scale data.
AI.method: Created Air Quality Arena (AQA) with two components: 1) AQA-Data - a large-scale multi-country, multi-pollutant dataset covering 6 major pollutants over 3 years across 7 diverse countries and 4 continents with 14,000+ station-pollutant series; 2) AQA-Bench - a comprehensive benchmark for air quality tasks. Evaluated 11 leading time series foundation models and classical baselines on short-term air quality forecasting.
AI.result: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) are effective zero-shot forecasters and consistently outperform classical baselines. The top-performing model uses a cross-modal architecture that leverages a vision foundation model for time series forecasting.
AI.conclusion: AQA addresses the limitations of existing benchmarks by providing the first comprehensive, large-scale air quality dataset and benchmark. The results show the effectiveness of TSFMs for air quality forecasting, with publicly released resources to advance research in this critical public health domain.

title: FormulaSPIN: Self-Play Fine-Tuning for Natural Language to Spreadsheet Formula Generation
comment: 15 pages,7 figures, 14 tables. Accepted to ACL 2026 Main Conference Oral
summary: Spreadsheet applications are used by hundreds of millions worldwide, yet writing formulas remains a significant barrier. Existing approaches rely on static supervised data, which quickly saturates on limited annotations. In this paper, we introduce FORMULASPIN, a self-play framework that breaks the ceiling of supervised fine-tuning by enabling iterative self-improvement without any additional data. Vanilla SPIN fails on this task: it uniformly penalizes every non-matching output, so execution-equivalent alternatives are punished as negatives in one example while serving as ground truth in another, producing contradictory gradients. Our framework resolves this by exploiting formula generation's unique advantage: binary executability provides implicit supervision that separates semantic errors from valid stylistic variants. We frame training as a two-player game in which the main player learns to prefer ground-truth formulas over those from its previous version, while execution feedback sorts outputs into distinct granularities-enabling an adaptive curriculum that shifts from semantic correctness to stylistic refinement. To further increase accuracy, we incorporate ExecVote, a semantic-level voting mechanism that naturally handles multiple valid formulations. Experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that FORMULASPIN achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 74.9% exact match and 87.1% execution accuracy on NL2FORMULA, matching models trained with additional preference annotations while outperforming both traditional SFT and frontier proprietary models. These findings underscore self-play's potential to tackle scarce data tasks and open the door to extending it beyond executable domains.

AI.tldr: FORMULASPIN is a self-play framework that improves formula generation in spreadsheets by using execution feedback to separate semantic errors from stylistic variants, achieving state-of-the-art performance without additional data.
AI.motivation: Spreadsheet formulas are widely used but difficult to write. Existing supervised approaches suffer from limited annotations and quickly saturate. There's a need for methods that can improve formula generation without requiring additional labeled data.
AI.method: FORMULASPIN introduces a self-play framework that treats training as a two-player game. It exploits formula executability as implicit supervision to separate semantic errors from stylistic variants. The approach includes ExecVote, a semantic-level voting mechanism to handle multiple valid formulations. It creates an adaptive curriculum that shifts from semantic correctness to stylistic refinement.
AI.result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance: 74.9% exact match and 87.1% execution accuracy on NL2FORMULA benchmark. It matches models trained with additional preference annotations while outperforming both traditional supervised fine-tuning and proprietary models.
AI.conclusion: Self-play has significant potential for tasks with scarce data. The approach successfully extends self-play to executable domains and demonstrates that binary executability can provide effective implicit supervision for iterative self-improvement without additional data.

title: Challenges of Explainability in Continual Learning for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Deep learning models have shown strong potential for time series forecasting, yet their deployment in real-world environmental monitoring remains challenging due to non-stationary dynamics and limited explainability. In this work, we investigate explainability as a central tool for understanding continual learning in adaptive time series forecasting, with Experience Replay strategies. We study neural forecasting architectures such as PatchMixer, PatchTST and DLinear, augmented with attention-based sampling mechanisms to support model adaptation over time. Explainability is leveraged through attention rollout and gradient-based attribution methods (Grad-CAM) to analyze both predictive behavior and sampling strategies within a continual learning framework. Experiments conducted on real-world piezometric time series exhibiting heterogeneous patterns and regime shifts show that analyzing model and sampling behaviors provides valuable insights into the dynamics of the continual learning framework. Beyond predictive performance, our results highlight the challenges and opportunities of using explainability to understand continual learning behaviors, revealing how attribution patterns evolve over time and how they can inform data selection and adaptation strategies in non-stationary forecasting scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates explainability techniques to understand continual learning behaviors in adaptive time series forecasting models, particularly focusing on how attention mechanisms and attribution methods reveal model adaptation patterns in non-stationary environmental monitoring scenarios.
AI.motivation: Deploying deep learning models for time series forecasting in real-world environmental monitoring is challenging due to non-stationary dynamics and limited explainability. The authors aim to use explainability as a tool to understand continual learning processes in adaptive forecasting models.
AI.method: The study combines neural forecasting architectures (PatchMixer, PatchTST, DLinear) with attention-based sampling mechanisms for model adaptation over time. Explainability is leveraged through attention rollout and gradient-based attribution methods (Grad-CAM) to analyze both predictive behavior and sampling strategies within a continual learning framework with Experience Replay strategies.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world piezometric time series with heterogeneous patterns and regime shifts show that analyzing model and sampling behaviors provides valuable insights into continual learning dynamics. The results reveal how attribution patterns evolve over time and how they can inform data selection and adaptation strategies in non-stationary forecasting scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Explainability provides critical insights into continual learning behaviors beyond predictive performance. The research highlights both challenges and opportunities in using attribution patterns to understand how models adapt over time, which can inform better data selection and adaptation strategies for non-stationary forecasting applications.

title: Information Discernment in Large Language Models
summary: LLMs are increasingly used with external knowledge sources like the internet. Do they weigh information appropriately -- updating more for reliable sources (source discernment) and more when claims bring priors closer to the truth (truth discernment)? We formalize this as information discernment and introduce Learn2Discern (L2D), an experimental framework and benchmark grounded in three normative axioms with interpretable metrics. To establish external validity, a pre-registered, quota-matched user study (n=299) confirms that real LLM users endorse all three axioms and report that violations reduce their trust and usage intent. Across 13 models and nearly 670K trials, we find consistent failures across both dimensions: models perform near chance on source and truth discernment, rely on source popularity twice as much as source reliability, and update roughly equally whether a claim improves or worsens their position relative to the ground truth. Models integrate external knowledge most effectively on datasets where their priors are already the most accurate. Newer and larger models improve truth discernment but not source discernment, a blind spot that model complexity does not address. We identify simple inference-time interventions that improve both forms of discernment. We release our dataset and survey as a testbed for a core alignment property that scales in importance as LLMs replace traditional search.

AI.tldr: LLMs fail at properly weighing external information - they don't distinguish between reliable/unreliable sources nor between truth-improving/truth-worsening claims, despite these being core alignment properties that real users value.
AI.motivation: As LLMs increasingly use external knowledge sources, it's crucial to assess whether they properly weight information by source reliability and truthfulness - a capability real users expect and value for trust in LLM systems.
AI.method: Introduced Learn2Discern (L2D) framework with three normative axioms and interpretable metrics; conducted pre-registered user study (n=299) to validate axioms; tested 13 models across 670K trials; analyzed source discernment, truth discernment, and update behaviors.
AI.result: Models perform near chance on source and truth discernment, rely 2x more on source popularity than reliability, update equally for truth-improving/worsening claims, and only integrate external knowledge effectively when priors are already accurate. Newer/larger models improve truth but not source discernment.
AI.conclusion: LLMs have serious deficiencies in information discernment despite user expectations; source discernment is a particular blind spot not solved by model scaling; simple interventions can help, highlighting the need for benchmarks addressing this core alignment property as LLMs replace search.

title: SUM: Unified Geometric Surgery on Spatio-Temporal Adaptation Vectors for Federated Class Incremental Learning
summary: Real-world intelligent systems often require both distributed collaboration across data-isolated clients and continual adaptation to evolving tasks. This setting naturally gives rise to Federated Class Incremental Learning (FCIL), which combines Federated Learning (FL) and Continual Learning (CL). However, their combination introduces two coupled sources of interference: spatial interference from heterogeneous clients and temporal interference from sequential tasks, jointly leading to Spatial-Temporal Catastrophic Forgetting (ST-CF). Existing approaches typically address spatial and temporal interference with separate mechanisms, often incurring additional client-side computation or communication, while leaving directional interactions among updates during aggregation unregulated. In this paper, we reinterpret FCIL as a unified multi-task learning problem, where both client and task updates are represented as adaptation vectors in a shared parameter space. Based on this view, we propose Unified Geometric Surgery on Spatio-Temporal Adaptation Vectors (SUM), a purely server-side framework that performs geometric surgery on adaptation vectors during aggregation. Spatial SUM mitigates client-level interference within each round, while causal online temporal SUM removes cross-task interference over time without additional client-side computation, communication, or memory beyond standard federated training. Empirically, SUM achieves up to 22% improvement over prior FCIL methods across diverse vision and language benchmarks while remaining robust to unreliable clients and maintaining computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: SUM is a server-only framework that treats Federated Class Incremental Learning (FCIL) as a unified multi-task problem, performing geometric surgery on client and task adaptation vectors to simultaneously address spatial and temporal interference without extra client-side costs.
AI.motivation: FCIL combines Federated Learning and Continual Learning, introducing coupled spatial interference (from heterogeneous clients) and temporal interference (from sequential tasks), leading to Spatial-Temporal Catastrophic Forgetting. Existing approaches address these separately with extra computation/communication costs and fail to regulate directional interactions during aggregation.
AI.method: Reinterprets FCIL as a unified multi-task learning problem where client and task updates are adaptation vectors in shared parameter space. Proposes SUM framework with two components: Spatial SUM mitigates client-level interference within each round, and causal online temporal SUM removes cross-task interference over time. Both operations are purely server-side during aggregation.
AI.result: Achieves up to 22% improvement over prior FCIL methods across diverse vision and language benchmarks. Remains robust to unreliable clients and maintains computational efficiency without additional client-side computation, communication, or memory beyond standard federated training.
AI.conclusion: By viewing FCIL as a multi-task problem and performing geometric surgery on adaptation vectors during server-side aggregation, SUM effectively addresses both spatial and temporal interference simultaneously, outperforming existing methods while being computationally efficient and client-friendly.

title: NEXUS: Structured Runtime Safety for Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary: Tool-using LLM agents increasingly execute high-impact actions, making runtime safety monitoring essential. We present NEXUS (Neural EXecution Utility and Safety), a structured-plan safety monitor that applies a formal intervention policy to select among four actions: allow, block, request confirmation, or request revision. NEXUS combines deterministic safety rules, argument-level inspection, and a calibrated logistic-regression risk score for graded escalation. On a 128-instance synthetic benchmark, NEXUS achieves an F1 score of 0.949 and a 4-class intervention accuracy of 0.6406, outperforming rule-only intervention selection by 27.3 percentage points. It also improves over rule-only on R-Judge (F1 = 0.861 vs. 0.849), matches rule-only on AgentHarm due to threat-model limits, and achieves 0% ASR at 99% control allow on IPI. On the rule-blind NEXUS-Stress benchmark, NEXUS reaches an F1 score of 0.881, highlighting the difficulty of fine-grained intervention routing. With 0.205 ms median latency, NEXUS adds under 0.1% overhead to typical agent loops. Code, benchmarks, and the calibrated risk scorer are publicly released.

AI.tldr: NEXUS is a safety monitoring system for tool-using LLM agents that uses structured plans, formal intervention policies, and calibrated risk scoring to make runtime safety decisions with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents increasingly execute high-impact actions in the real world, runtime safety monitoring becomes essential to prevent harmful or unintended consequences.
AI.method: NEXUS combines deterministic safety rules, argument-level inspection, and a calibrated logistic-regression risk score to make graded intervention decisions (allow, block, request confirmation, or request revision) based on structured agent plans.
AI.result: Achieved 0.949 F1 score on synthetic benchmark, 0.6406 intervention accuracy, outperforming rule-only approaches by 27.3 percentage points. Also showed strong performance on R-Judge (F1=0.861), AgentHarm, and IPI benchmarks while maintaining only 0.205ms median latency (under 0.1% overhead).
AI.conclusion: NEXUS demonstrates that combining rule-based and ML-based approaches enables effective, low-overhead safety monitoring for LLM agents, balancing safety with utility through calibrated risk assessment and formal intervention policies.

title: STN-TGAT: Top-K Portfolio Construction via Prior-Guided Graph Attention with Learnable Soft-Threshold Sparsification
summary: This paper tackles the problem of stock ranking and portfolio construction under realistic investment settings by jointly modeling temporal dynamics and cross-sectional dependencies. We propose the Soft-Threshold NMI-prior Transformer Graph Attention Network (STN-TGAT), which integrates a temporal Transformer with a Graph Attention Network to capture long-horizon sequential patterns and dynamic inter-stock relationships. An NMI-based prior graph combined with a soft-threshold sparsification mechanism enhances structural robustness by mitigating noisy correlations while preserving informative connections. The portfolio formation process incorporates practical considerations, including Top-5 selection within the Top-50 $S\&P$ 500 constituents, explicit weight allocation, and transaction cost adjustment, thereby aligning the evaluation with real-world trading conditions. Empirical results on real-world data demonstrate that STN-TGAT consistently outperforms benchmark models from predictive accuracy and investment profitability measured by portfolio returns. These findings suggest that combining decision-aligned training with adaptive relational modeling provides a coherent and practically effective framework for data-driven portfolio construction.

AI.tldr: STN-TGAT: A Transformer-Graph Attention Network for stock ranking and portfolio construction that captures temporal dynamics and cross-stock dependencies with practical investment constraints.
AI.motivation: Current portfolio construction methods need to better handle realistic investment settings by jointly modeling temporal patterns and cross-sectional dependencies while accounting for practical constraints like transaction costs and portfolio selection rules.
AI.method: Proposed STN-TGAT integrates temporal Transformer with Graph Attention Network to capture sequential patterns and dynamic inter-stock relationships. Uses NMI-based prior graph with soft-threshold sparsification to reduce noise. Incorporates practical portfolio formation: Top-5 selection within Top-50 S&P 500, explicit weight allocation, and transaction cost adjustment.
AI.result: STN-TGAT consistently outperforms benchmark models in both predictive accuracy and investment profitability (portfolio returns) on real-world data.
AI.conclusion: Combining decision-aligned training with adaptive relational modeling provides a coherent and practically effective framework for data-driven portfolio construction.

title: Stochastic Primal-Dual Decoding for Multiobjective Generative Recommender Systems
summary:
Recent advances in recommender systems (RS) have shown substantial performance gains through generative modelling. In practice, recommendation often involves constructing slates -- ordered lists of items -- that must satisfy multiple objectives beyond relevance, such as constraints defined over item attributes or fairness constraints. Existing multiobjective approaches either rely on post-processing techniques designed for non-generative settings, or incorporate auxiliary objectives directly into model training. The former does not explicitly account for the sequential nature of generative RS, while the latter is often impractical in large-scale systems.
  We propose a lightweight, inference-time decoding layer that augments autoregressive generative RS to support multiobjective slate generation without modifying or retraining the underlying model. We formulate decoding as an online constrained optimisation problem, where items are selected sequentially, and trade-offs between relevance and auxiliary objectives are adjusted dynamically based on the remaining constraint slack, i.e., how much of each objective remains to be satisfied. This is implemented via a stochastic primal-dual approximation scheme that balances relevance and auxiliary objectives during generation.
  We provide theoretical guarantees on constraint violation and regret, and evaluate the proposed approach through extensive offline experiments and a large-scale online A/B experiment in a real-world recommender system. Our results show consistent improvements in multiobjective trade-offs, including a +1.8\% gain in the auxiliary objectives achieved at zero cost to user satisfaction.

AI.tldr: A lightweight inference-time decoding layer for generative recommender systems that enables multiobjective slate generation without retraining the base model, using online constrained optimization with stochastic primal-dual approximation.
AI.motivation: Real-world recommender systems need to construct slates (ordered lists) that satisfy multiple objectives beyond relevance, such as fairness constraints and item attribute requirements. Existing multiobjective approaches either use post-processing techniques not designed for generative RS or require model retraining, which is impractical in large-scale systems.
AI.method: Proposes a lightweight inference-time decoding layer that augments autoregressive generative RS without modifying/retraining the base model. Formulates decoding as an online constrained optimization problem where items are selected sequentially, with trade-offs between relevance and auxiliary objectives adjusted dynamically based on remaining constraint slack. Uses a stochastic primal-dual approximation scheme to balance objectives during generation.
AI.result: Extensive offline experiments and large-scale online A/B test in a real-world recommender system show consistent improvements in multiobjective trade-offs, including +1.8% gain in auxiliary objectives achieved at zero cost to user satisfaction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed inference-time decoding layer provides an effective, practical solution for multiobjective slate generation in generative recommender systems, offering theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements without requiring model retraining or compromising user satisfaction.

title: Building Fast, Evaluating Slow: Pipeline Choices Dominate Autointerpretability Score Variance
summary: Cross-paper comparison of sparse autoencoder (SAE) interpretability often relies on autointerpretability scores. In this evaluation pipeline, a language model (LM) explains each feature, and another LM scores the explanation. For these comparisons to be meaningful, scores must reflect stable properties of the features rather than confounding aspects of the evaluation pipeline. Through systematic experiments across four metrics (simulation, detection, fuzzing, purity), two models (Pythia-160M, Apertus-8B), and four axes of methodological variation, we show that this assumption does not hold. Specifically, we find that R1) methodological variance collectively exceeds architectural variance across all metrics and tested models; R2) each metric exhibits a distinct instability profile, with detection being the most stable and fuzzing unreliable across all conditions; R3) top-k feature rankings do not stay consistent across corpus and draw conditions, masking per-feature instability behind stable mean scores; a failure that cannot be detected by monitoring explanation similarity alone. These findings suggest that cross-paper comparisons based on autointerpretability scores may reflect pipeline differences rather than architectural differences, with implications for the ongoing debate on SAE utility. More broadly, unreliable evaluation slows progress in interpretability research at a time when reliable tools for understanding AI systems are needed. To support evaluation, we contribute a variance decomposition approach, a Stability Check, and a Minimum Reporting Checklist.

AI.tldr: Current autointerpretability scoring for sparse autoencoders shows significant methodological instability that can outweigh architectural differences, undermining cross-paper comparisons of SAE interpretability.
AI.motivation: Cross-paper comparisons of sparse autoencoder (SAE) interpretability rely on autointerpretability scores, but it's unclear whether these scores reflect stable feature properties or pipeline artifacts, potentially misleading the SAE utility debate.
AI.method: Systematic experiments across four metrics (simulation, detection, fuzzing, purity), two models (Pythia-160M, Apertus-8B), and four axes of methodological variation, plus development of variance decomposition, Stability Check, and Minimum Reporting Checklist.
AI.result: 1) Methodological variance exceeds architectural variance across all metrics/models; 2) Each metric has distinct instability (detection most stable, fuzzing unreliable); 3) Top-k feature rankings are inconsistent across conditions despite stable mean scores; 4) Explanation similarity alone can't detect this failure.
AI.conclusion: Cross-paper comparisons based on autointerpretability scores may reflect pipeline differences rather than architectural differences, undermining SAE utility debates. The paper contributes evaluation tools (variance decomposition, Stability Check, Minimum Reporting Checklist) to address these reliability issues in interpretability research.

title: LISA: Linear-Indexed Sparse Attention for Efficient Long-Context Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in long chain-of-thought reasoning models such as DeepSeek-R1 have led to increasingly longer inference context lengths under the test-time scaling paradigm. However, the O(n^2) computational complexity of standard self-attention causes inference costs to grow sharply with long sequences, limiting the deployment of long-CoT reasoning in production settings. To address this, we propose LISA (Linear-Indexed Sparse Attention), a plug-and-play attention replacement module that requires no pretraining from scratch. LISA integrates two lightweight components in parallel within the original model: (1) a Linear Attention module that provides long-range memory with O(n) time complexity; (2) a Lightning Indexer that selects the top-M important tokens from the full context to feed into a Sparse Self-Attention. The two branches are fused via a gating mechanism, reducing inference complexity from O(n^2) to O(nM) (M << n) for generating n tokens. We design a two-stage training pipeline: Stage 1 initializes the model by integrating the linear attention to capture long-range dependencies, complemented by a sliding-window attention mechanism that is optimized via knowledge distillation to approximate the full self-attention distribution of a frozen teacher model. In Stage 2, we further introduce the Indexer to replace the static sliding-window mechanism, enabling dynamic token selection from broader contexts. The Indexer is trained using a novel per-head KL divergence loss, which aligns its selection behavior with the attention patterns of the teacher model. Experiments on DeepSeek-distilled-Qwen models demonstrate that LISA achieves a 50% inference speedup under 16K-token context, while improving average performance by 5.6% on reasoning benchmarks including AIME and MATH-500.

AI.tldr: LISA (Linear-Indexed Sparse Attention) is a plug-and-play attention replacement that reduces inference complexity from O(n²) to O(nM) for long chain-of-thought reasoning models, achieving 50% speedup with 5.6% performance improvement.
AI.motivation: Standard self-attention's O(n²) computational complexity causes sharply growing inference costs with long sequences, limiting deployment of long chain-of-thought reasoning models in production settings despite recent advances in test-time scaling.
AI.method: LISA integrates two parallel components: (1) Linear Attention module for long-range memory with O(n) complexity, and (2) Lightning Indexer that selects top-M important tokens for Sparse Self-Attention. Uses two-stage training: Stage 1 initializes via linear attention + sliding-window mechanism optimized via knowledge distillation; Stage 2 introduces Indexer for dynamic token selection trained with per-head KL divergence loss.
AI.result: Experiments on DeepSeek-distilled-Qwen models show LISA achieves 50% inference speedup under 16K-token context while improving average performance by 5.6% on reasoning benchmarks including AIME and MATH-500.
AI.conclusion: LISA successfully addresses the computational bottleneck of long chain-of-thought reasoning by providing a plug-and-play attention replacement that significantly reduces inference complexity while maintaining or improving reasoning performance, making long-CoT reasoning more practical for production deployment.

title: Scale-Aware Learning of Chaotic Dynamics on Unstructured Meshes via Binned Spectral Losses
summary: Surrogate modeling for high-dimensional nonlinear dynamical systems that exhibit chaos requires mechanisms that preserve not only pointwise accuracy but also the scale-dependent structure of physical fields. Bandwise spectral power losses, such as the binned spectral loss function, provide such supervision on structured grids, where Fourier modes define a standard frequency decomposition. On irregular meshes, however, no canonical Fourier basis exists, and spectral representations must be constructed from graph operators induced by mesh connectivity and geometry. In this study, we extend the binned spectral power loss for application to unstructured-mesh surrogate modeling of nonlinear dynamical systems. This is obtained by replacing Fourier bands with graph-Laplacian frequency bands, and we provide scalable Chebyshev and multilevel approximations for improving long-horizon rollout fidelity. In its full-spectrum form, our approach uses graph Laplacian eigenspaces to provide a graph analogue of Fourier band-power matching, but incurs the high cost of spectral decomposition. As a scalable approximation, we replace exact band projectors with sparse Chebyshev polynomial graph filters, avoiding explicit eigendecomposition. When utilizing multilevel graph architectures, we introduce Graph Laplacian Energy Alignment for Meshes (GLEAM), which applies retained-subspace scale-aware supervision across graph hierarchies so that coarse and fine representations are regularized during autoregressive rollout. Our results show that the proposed spectral losses improve long-horizon rollout fidelity and preserve statistical invariants for the forecasting of turbulent flows on unstructured meshes, compared to deterministic baselines.

AI.tldr: Extends spectral loss functions from structured to unstructured meshes using graph Laplacians, improving surrogate model fidelity for chaotic systems.
AI.motivation: Existing surrogate modeling techniques for chaotic systems on structured grids use spectral loss functions that supervise scale-dependent structure, but these methods don't extend to irregular/unstructured meshes where no canonical Fourier basis exists.
AI.method: Proposes graph-Laplacian frequency bands to replace Fourier bands, with two scalable approximations: 1) Chebyshev polynomial graph filters to avoid eigendecomposition, and 2) GLEAM (Graph Laplacian Energy Alignment for Meshes) for multilevel graph architectures that applies scale-aware supervision across hierarchies.
AI.result: The spectral losses improve long-horizon rollout fidelity and preserve statistical invariants for turbulent flow forecasting on unstructured meshes compared to deterministic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Graph spectral approaches effectively extend spectral supervision to unstructured meshes, enabling accurate surrogate modeling of high-dimensional nonlinear chaotic systems on irregular grids while maintaining scalability.

title: Profile-Graph Memory for LLM Agents: Implicit Cross-Entity Traversal through Narrative Profiles
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables. Code and MemHop benchmark: https://github.com/ShengtongZhu/ProGraph
summary: Long-term memory is essential for LLM agents that interact across sessions, yet current memory benchmarks primarily evaluate single-hop recall, leaving multi-hop association largely unmeasured. We make three contributions. First, we introduce MemHop, a multi-hop memory benchmark of 1,000 questions at hop depths 1-5 across 10 social-network scenarios, with per-hop evidence annotations. Second, we present Profile-Graph Memory (ProGraph), a two-layer memory architecture combining (i) profile expansion -- substring-matched traversal of entity names that naturally appear in LLM-written profile narratives, a minimal alternative to explicit knowledge-graph construction -- and (ii) compression residuals -- exact dates, quantities, and named items co-extracted with each profile update at zero extra API cost. Third, a full-grid ablation shows cross-benchmark mechanism specialization: profile expansion drives multi-hop reasoning (-22.6pp on MemHop when removed) while compression residuals drive precision recall (-8.6pp on LoCoMo when not co-extracted), with cross-effects under 3pp within a single architecture. ProGraph averages 80.1% on MemHop (matching the FullContext reference) and 78.4% on LoCoMo (exceeding FullContext by 11.3pp), outperforming Mem0, A-Mem, HippoRAG, and RAG on both. We release MemHop, ProGraph, and baseline implementations.

AI.tldr: ProGraph is a two-layer memory architecture combining profile expansion and compression residuals that achieves state-of-the-art performance on multi-hop memory benchmarks while matching FullContext reference on MemHop and exceeding it on LoCoMo.
