AI.motivation: Current memory benchmarks for LLM agents primarily evaluate single-hop recall but leave multi-hop association largely unmeasured. There's a need for better evaluation of memory systems that can handle complex multi-hop reasoning across sessions.
AI.method: Two-layer architecture: (1) Profile expansion using substring-matched traversal of entity names in LLM-written profile narratives (minimal alternative to explicit knowledge graphs), and (2) Compression residuals that co-extract exact dates, quantities, and named items with each profile update at zero extra API cost.
AI.result: ProGraph achieves 80.1% on MemHop (matching FullContext reference) and 78.4% on LoCoMo (exceeding FullContext by 11.3pp). It outperforms Mem0, A-Mem, HippoRAG, and RAG on both benchmarks. Ablation studies show profile expansion drives multi-hop reasoning (-22.6pp drop on MemHop when removed) while compression residuals drive precision recall (-8.6pp on LoCoMo when not co-extracted).
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces MemHop benchmark for multi-hop memory evaluation and presents ProGraph as an effective memory architecture that combines profile-based association with efficient residual extraction. The approach demonstrates strong performance across different types of memory tasks with specialized mechanisms for different reasoning needs.

title: Neural Operator Surrogates for Two-Dimensional Neutron Flux Estimation
comment: to be submitted to ANS Winter 2026
summary: This work extends our one-dimensional single-sweep neural-operator studies to two dimensions. We consider one-group transport with isotropic scattering. As in the one-dimensional work, we use Fourier neural operators (FNOs) to approximate the high-fidelity scalar flux. Additionally, we also investigate U-shaped neural operators (UNOs) in this study. We consider three surrogates. The first two map the material and source fields directly to the flux, one using an FNO and one using a UNO. The third is an FNO that additionally takes the scalar flux after one source iteration, the single-sweep approximation, as an input. Each case is solved to high fidelity with a verified discrete-ordinates solver, and an average relative L_2 error norm is used to characterize the quality of the inferred maps. We train every surrogate over three random seeds so that differences between them can be assessed against run-to-run variability. Two questions guide the study: whether the single-sweep input improves accuracy over the direct maps, and whether training on the logarithm of the flux improves accuracy in the strongly attenuated regions relevant to shielding.

AI.tldr: The paper extends single-sweep neural operators from 1D to 2D for transport problems, comparing Fourier neural operators (FNOs) and U-shaped neural operators (UNOs) with different input configurations and training strategies.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to extend previous 1D single-sweep neural operator studies to 2D transport problems with isotropic scattering, specifically investigating whether incorporating single-sweep approximations improves accuracy and whether logarithmic training enhances results in shielded regions.
AI.method: The method involves three surrogate models: (1) FNO mapping material/source fields directly to flux, (2) UNO mapping material/source fields directly to flux, and (3) FNO that additionally takes the single-sweep approximation as input. All models are trained on high-fidelity discrete-ordinates solver data using average relative L₂ error, with training repeated over three random seeds for statistical significance.
AI.result: While the abstract doesn't present specific numerical results, it establishes the experimental framework to answer two key questions: whether single-sweep input improves accuracy over direct mapping, and whether logarithmic training improves accuracy in strongly attenuated shielding regions.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a systematic approach to evaluating neural operators for 2D transport problems, focusing on comparative analysis of different architectures and training strategies for accuracy improvements in shielding applications.

title: Lifted Representation Hypothesis in Language Models
comment: 20 pages, 6 figures, ICML 2026 Workshop: Philosophy Meets Machine Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often answer queries by mapping individual observations to more general rule-like structures. However, it remains unclear how these structures are stored, selected, and revised. To study this process, we propose thelifted representation hypothesis: LLMs update memory through shared latent structures rather than isolated instance-level facts. This view frames lifting as an efficient use of symmetry across instances, and shattering as the refinement of coarse lifted structures into more specific subtypes. We evaluate LLMs' lifting and shattering through controlled exception-learning experiments across in-context learning, LoRA, and full fine-tuning. We find that LLMs are vulnerable to shattering failures when data are governed by nested rules and exceptions, while lifting often occurs prematurely. These results highlight the need to study the relation between data and rule structures in LLMs.

AI.tldr: LLMs use latent rule structures ("lifting") to generalize from observations, but struggle with nested rules and exceptions ("shattering failures"), revealing limitations in how they store and revise rule knowledge.
AI.motivation: While LLMs can map observations to general rule structures, the mechanisms of how these structures are stored, selected, and revised remain poorly understood. The authors aim to study this cognitive process in LLMs.
AI.method: Proposes the "lifted representation hypothesis" - that LLMs update memory through shared latent structures rather than isolated facts. They evaluate LLMs' lifting and shattering capabilities through controlled exception-learning experiments across three training regimes: in-context learning, LoRA, and full fine-tuning.
AI.result: LLMs are vulnerable to "shattering failures" when data follow nested rules with exceptions, and often engage in "premature lifting" - oversimplifying before fully capturing exceptions. These patterns were consistent across different training methods.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals fundamental limitations in LLMs' rule-learning mechanisms, particularly in handling exceptions within hierarchical rule structures. This highlights the need for better understanding of how data and rule structures interact in LLMs to improve their reasoning capabilities.

title: The Orthogonalized Read Is a Removable Training Scaffold for Recurrent Memory
comment: 18 pages, 8 figures. Code, per-seed results, and checkpoints: https://github.com/no-way-labs/recurrent-memory-scaffold
summary: A recent report finds that orthogonalizing the mLSTM memory matrix at read time (five Newton-Schulz iterations, trained through) substantially improves noisy associative recall. The effect replicates, but it is not a memory improvement. Training on this task is a long chance plateau followed by a sharp escape, and the orthogonalized read acts by re-conditioning the learning problem during the plateau. Three properties establish this. It must be self-consistent: an exact recursive least-squares read (the Mesa layer) reproduces it, while straight-through halves, delta-rule writes, frozen random keys, and plain normalization all fail. It is uniform: across a learning-rate x hardness grid it multiplies the escape hazard roughly six-fold with no detectable hardness dependence, widening the workable learning-rate corridor that narrows for the baseline. And it is removable: applied to failed models at inference it rescues none, and annealed away on an escape-triggered schedule it leaves numerically stock mLSTMs at full accuracy. Much of the published gain needs no architecture at all -- solved-rate at a fixed budget measures escape hazard, which follows a heat/noise law (learning-rate elasticity +3.0, gradient-noise elasticity -1.65) under which the original vocab-96 result is a large-batch noise condition rather than a capacity one. Decoding the memory state directly shows failed models carry roughly half their associations in linearly recoverable form: the plateau is a readout failure over half-written storage. Two conclusions travel beyond the intervention: recall benchmarks used for architecture selection partly measure trainability, and the system is a fully instrumented model organism of "emergence," in which a sharp behavioral threshold demonstrably arises from a censored metric over gradually accumulating structure.

AI.tldr: Orthogonalizing mLSTM memory matrix at read time improves noisy associative recall, but the gain comes from re-conditioning the learning problem rather than enhancing memory capacity.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates why orthogonalizing mLSTM memory matrix improves noisy associative recall, aiming to understand whether this represents a genuine memory improvement or a training dynamics effect.
AI.method: Analyzes orthogonalization intervention through empirical tests: self-consistency (exact recursive least-squares read reproduces effect), uniformity (consistent escape hazard across conditions), and removability (intervention can be annealed away after training). Also examines learning dynamics and memory state decoding.
AI.result: Orthogonalization multiplies escape hazard ~6x, widens workable learning rate corridor, and rescues no failed models at inference. Failed models carry ~50% associations in linearly recoverable form - plateau is readout failure over half-written storage. Much published gain measures escape hazard rather than capacity.
AI.conclusion: Orthogonalization's benefit comes from re-conditioning learning problem, not memory improvement. Recall benchmarks partly measure trainability, and the system serves as a model organism for "emergence" where sharp thresholds arise from censored metrics over gradual structural accumulation.

title: GraphContainer: A Unified Platform for Comparing and Debugging Graph RAG Methods
summary: Graph RAG mitigates hallucinations and stale knowledge in LLMs, particularly for multi-hop question answering. However, existing approaches remain highly fragmented and incompatible. The structural heterogeneity of graph formats across different frameworks and the lack of granular visualization tools make it exceedingly difficult to evaluate and compare retrieval behaviors. To bridge this gap, we propose GraphContainer, a novel platform designed to unify and visualize diverse graph RAG workflows. GraphContainer features two key components: (1) a Unified Graph Representation (UGR) layer that seamlessly standardizes multi-format graphs, and (2) a Graph Recorder that tracks and visually renders the step-by-step retrieval process. Through an interactive web interface, we demonstrate GraphContainer's ability to import heterogeneous graphs and perform live, traceable visual debugging of graph RAG methods. Ultimately, we show how GraphContainer enables controlled comparisons of various graph formats and retrieval strategies, lowering the barrier for researchers and practitioners to design optimal graph RAG pipelines. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/O02eNJLwkU0.

AI.tldr: GraphContainer is a unified platform for standardizing and visualizing diverse graph RAG workflows, addressing fragmentation and incompatibility issues in existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing graph RAG approaches for mitigating LLM hallucinations are highly fragmented and incompatible due to structural heterogeneity in graph formats across different frameworks. The lack of granular visualization tools makes it difficult to evaluate and compare retrieval behaviors.
AI.method: Proposed GraphContainer with two key components: (1) Unified Graph Representation (UGR) layer that standardizes multi-format graphs, and (2) Graph Recorder that tracks and visually renders step-by-step retrieval processes. Features an interactive web interface for importing heterogeneous graphs and performing live, traceable visual debugging.
AI.result: GraphContainer enables controlled comparisons of various graph formats and retrieval strategies, lowering barriers for researchers and practitioners to design optimal graph RAG pipelines. Demonstration video shows its ability to work with heterogeneous graphs.
AI.conclusion: GraphContainer successfully addresses the fragmentation problem in graph RAG workflows by providing a unified platform for standardization and visualization, making it easier to evaluate, compare, and optimize retrieval behaviors across different approaches.

title: LAARA: Layer-Aware Adaptive Rank Allocation for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation is widely used for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, yet existing methods typically assign the same adapter rank to every transformer layer despite their heterogeneous adaptation requirements. In this work, we show theoretically and empirically that uniform rank allocation is fundamentally suboptimal. Motivated by this observation, we propose LAARA (Layer Aware Adaptive Rank Allocation framework), a search-free framework that dynamically allocates ranks using lightweight diagonal Fisher estimates computed during training. LAARA combines projection-wise normalization, logarithmic compression, blended adapter importance estimation, and a vote-to-change dampening mechanism to produce stable and efficient rank adaptation. Experiments on GLUE and MathInstruct benchmark demonstrate that LAARA consistently matches or outperforms popular state of the art approaches such as LoRA, AdaLoRA, DyLoRA, and Bitfit while using significantly fewer trainable parameters. Our results show that Fisher-guided rank allocation provides a principled and effective foundation for adaptive parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The code is publicly available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/LAARA-D305/LAARA.py

AI.tldr: LAARA is a search-free framework for adaptive rank allocation in LoRA fine-tuning that dynamically assigns different ranks to each transformer layer using Fisher information, outperforming uniform rank methods with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing LoRA methods typically use uniform rank allocation across all transformer layers, ignoring their heterogeneous adaptation requirements. Theoretical and empirical analysis shows this uniform approach is fundamentally suboptimal for parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
AI.method: LAARA uses lightweight diagonal Fisher estimates computed during training to dynamically allocate ranks to each layer. It combines projection-wise normalization, logarithmic compression, blended adapter importance estimation, and a vote-to-change dampening mechanism for stable and efficient rank adaptation.
AI.result: Experiments on GLUE and MathInstruct benchmarks show LAARA consistently matches or outperforms state-of-the-art approaches like LoRA, AdaLoRA, DyLoRA, and Bitfit while using significantly fewer trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: Fisher-guided rank allocation provides a principled and effective foundation for adaptive parameter-efficient fine-tuning, demonstrating that layer-aware adaptive rank allocation is superior to uniform approaches.

title: AdaRoPE: Not All Attention Heads Should Rotate and Scale Equally
summary: Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) is widely adopted in Transformers to encode positional information, yet standard implementations enforce a uniform frequency schedule and scaling across all attention heads. Using simplified retrieval tasks and length generalization scenarios, we show -- both empirically and theoretically -- that heads with different functional roles require distinct frequency ranges and attention scaling factors to operate effectively. Ignoring this structure leads to suboptimal utilization of embedding dimensions and degraded performance, particularly under long-context settings. To address these limitations, we propose AdaRoPE, which equips each attention head with learnable rotation frequencies and attention scaling factors. Pretrained LLMs with AdaRoPE consistently outperform existing RoPE variants, including partial RoPE and NoPE baselines. For context extension, we further show that uniform frequency and attention scaling, used in methods such as YaRN, are suboptimal. By applying head-specific scaling, AdaRoPE enables better context extension while better preserving short-context performance in both the extrapolation setting and the long-context continued pretraining setting. These results highlight the importance of optimizing rotary position embedding at the level of individual attention heads.

AI.tldr: AdaRoPE introduces learnable rotation frequencies and attention scaling factors per attention head in rotary position embeddings, improving performance and context extension compared to uniform RoPE implementations.
AI.motivation: Standard RoPE implementations use uniform frequency schedules across all attention heads, ignoring that different heads have different functional roles requiring distinct frequency ranges and scaling factors. This leads to suboptimal dimension utilization and degraded performance, especially in long-context settings.
AI.method: Proposes AdaRoPE which makes rotation frequencies and attention scaling factors learnable parameters for each attention head, allowing heads to specialize based on their functional needs.
AI.result: AdaRoPE outperforms existing RoPE variants in pretrained LLMs. For context extension, head-specific scaling enables better long-context performance while preserving short-context capabilities, outperforming uniform scaling methods like YaRN.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing rotary position embeddings at the individual attention head level is crucial for better performance, as different heads require distinct frequency ranges and scaling factors based on their functional roles.

title: Predicting Groundwater Arsenic Concentrations Using Graph Neural Networks
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures, 1 table, AAAI '26 AI4ES workshop acceptance
summary: Arsenic contamination in groundwater presents a longstanding public health crisis in the United States, especially for households depending on private wells. Accurate and spatially informed prediction of arsenic concentration is vital to identify high-risk areas and focus mitigation efforts. However, there is a lack of generalizable models for representing continuous variation in arsenic concentrations across regions. In this work, we pose arsenic prediction as a regression task and construct a spatially integrated dataset to aggregate over 74,000 arsenic samples from the Water Quality Portal (WQP), Mineral Resources Data System (MRDS), and Gridded National Soil Survey Geographic Database (gNATSGO). Specifically, we use a variety of techniques including kNearest Neighbors (k-NN) and Geographic Information Systems (GIS) to join arsenic measurement points from across the United States by location. Building on this dataset, we evaluate a diverse suite of machine learning models, including tree-based ensemble approaches, multilayer perceptrons, and spatially aware graph neural networks (GNN). Our findings show that while gradient-boosted trees are still considered state-of-the-art in the field of tabular data, GNNs are able to further account for spatial dependence to match or outperform the results of gradient-boosted trees. These results demonstrate that graph-based and spatially informed learning can enhance environmental prediction and provide a foundation for improved groundwater risk mapping and monitoring.

AI.tldr: The paper develops machine learning models to predict arsenic concentrations in US groundwater by integrating spatial data from multiple sources and showing that graph neural networks can match or outperform traditional gradient-boosted trees by accounting for spatial dependencies.
AI.motivation: Arsenic contamination in groundwater is a major public health crisis in the US, especially for private well users. There's a need for accurate, spatially informed prediction models to identify high-risk areas and focus mitigation efforts, but current models lack generalizability and ability to represent continuous spatial variation across regions.
AI.method: Created a spatially integrated dataset aggregating over 74,000 arsenic samples from three sources (WQP, MRDS, gNATSGO). Used k-NN and GIS techniques to join measurement points by location. Evaluated various ML models including tree-based ensembles, multilayer perceptrons, and spatially aware graph neural networks (GNNs).
AI.result: While gradient-boosted trees are still state-of-the-art for tabular data, GNNs are able to further account for spatial dependence and match or outperform gradient-boosted trees' results. GNNs demonstrate enhanced performance by incorporating spatial relationships.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based and spatially informed learning can enhance environmental prediction for arsenic contamination, providing a foundation for improved groundwater risk mapping and monitoring. Spatially aware models like GNNs offer advantages over traditional approaches by better capturing spatial dependencies.

title: Statistically Grounded Sparse-Feature Interventions for Activation-Space Control in Large Language Models
summary: Activation steering offers a lightweight alternative to fine-tuning for behavioral control of large language models, but SAE-based steering methods often rely on learned steering objectives or single-criterion feature selection. We introduce a transparent SAE-feature steering pipeline that first applies a six-condition reliability filter, then ranks sparse features through an unweighted Borda consensus over three complementary statistics: $F$-test, KSG mutual information, and Cohen's $d$. The resulting steering direction is constructed as a Cohen's-$d$-weighted combination of SAE decoder rows, providing an optimization-free direction motivated by Fisher-LDA under approximate SAE-feature decorrelation. Across three Gemma-family models, four behavioral domains, and 356 layer-strength configurations, the method produces measurable domain-specific shifts while revealing a substantial gap between raw attribute movement and quality-preserving generation. In the strongest configuration, logical-correctness steering reaches a primary-score delta of $+1.16$ in Gemma~2 9B; however, our broader finding is that usable steering is highly localized by model, domain, layer, and strength. These results argue that activation-steering evaluations should report quality-conditioned success alongside raw behavioral shift. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/Oshayer-Siddique/LLM-Steering-Using-SAE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a transparent SAE-feature steering pipeline for LLMs that uses a reliability filter and multi-criteria feature ranking without optimization, achieving measurable behavioral shifts but showing steering success is highly context-dependent.
AI.motivation: Current SAE-based steering methods often rely on learned steering objectives or single-criterion feature selection, lacking transparency and robustness. There's a need for more interpretable steering approaches that don't require optimization while effectively controlling LLM behavior.
AI.method: A transparent pipeline that: 1) applies a six-condition reliability filter to SAE features, 2) ranks features using unweighted Borda consensus over three statistics (F-test, KSG mutual information, Cohen's d), and 3) constructs steering directions as Cohen's-d-weighted combination of SAE decoder rows, inspired by Fisher-LDA under approximate feature decorrelation.
AI.result: The method produces measurable domain-specific shifts across three Gemma-family models, four behavioral domains, and 356 configurations. Best performance achieved +1.16 primary-score delta in logical correctness for Gemma 2 9B. However, steering success is highly localized by model, domain, layer, and strength, revealing a gap between attribute movement and quality-preserving generation.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering evaluations should report quality-conditioned success alongside raw behavioral shift. Usable steering is highly context-dependent, requiring careful selection of model, domain, layer, and strength parameters rather than one-size-fits-all solutions.

title: Decodable but Not Detectable: A Leakage Fingerprint for Near-OOD Benchmarks
summary: While auditing a perturbation-based OOD detector on a document benchmark, we recorded an AUROC of 0.326 -- well below the 0.5 chance level. The cause is a benchmark leak: the designated "OOD" class is one the model was trained on, so its examples sit inside the in-distribution fit set and the detector is penalized for correctly ranking them as familiar. Deleting the class and retraining 35 models across two domains raises the score to 0.911. We distill the contamination into a leak fingerprint -- near-perfect supervised decodability (AUROC approximately 1) coupled with unsupervised detection collapsed below 0.65 -- and validate it on a controlled battery of 52 settings (20 leaked, 32 clean) across ResNet-50 and ViT-B/16 on CIFAR-10/100, achieving sensitivity 18/20 and specificity 31/32 in embedding space; the matched fit-set-exclusion controls are perfect at 20/20. An in-the-wild audit of 24 standard near/far OOD benchmark pairs fires on exactly one (the intrinsically hard CIFAR-100 vs CIFAR-10 pair) and on no far-OOD pair, confirming specificity and that standard cross-dataset construction is clean. Under the corrected protocol, perturbation signals are decodable but not detectable: a supervised reader recovers the OOD signal (AUROC 0.87-1.00) while no unsupervised detector does, and the perturbation method does not improve on plain Mahalanobis distance. We provide a theoretical account of why and, for transparency, retract an earlier circular correlation. The contributions are a corrected protocol and a validated leak diagnostic, not a new OOD method.

AI.tldr: Paper exposes dataset contamination in OOD evaluation benchmarks where designated "OOD" class was actually in the training data, causing detectors to be penalized for correctly identifying familiar samples. Authors provide diagnostic fingerprint for detecting such leaks and retract earlier findings based on contaminated benchmarks.
AI.motivation: During auditing of perturbation-based OOD detectors, authors discovered suspiciously low AUROC scores (0.326 vs 0.5 chance level), suggesting potential benchmark contamination where "OOD" classes were actually part of the training distribution.
AI.method: Developed a leak fingerprint diagnostic based on supervised decodability (AUROC ≈1) coupled with collapsed unsupervised detection (AUROC <0.65). Validated on 52 controlled settings across ResNet-50 and ViT-B/16 on CIFAR-10/100 datasets, and audited 24 standard OOD benchmark pairs in the wild.
AI.result: After correcting contamination, AUROC improved from 0.326 to 0.911. Leak fingerprint achieved sensitivity 18/20 and specificity 31/32. Found standard cross-dataset constructions are clean except for CIFAR-100 vs CIFAR-10 pair. Under corrected protocol, perturbation signals are decodable but not detectable, and perturbation methods don't improve over plain Mahalanobis distance.
AI.conclusion: Paper provides a validated diagnostic for detecting benchmark contamination in OOD evaluation and corrects previously flawed evaluation protocols. Main contributions are methodological: identifying and fixing evaluation issues rather than proposing new OOD detection methods. Authors retract earlier circular correlation findings based on contaminated data.

title: Logic-Guided Data Extraction with Answer Set Programming and Large Language Models
comment: 42nd International Conference on Logic Programming, to appear in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP)
summary: When Large Language Models (LLMs) are used for semantic data extraction from unstructured text, producing candidate relational facts from natural language, they may remain unreliable for tasks requiring complex combinatorial reasoning and global consistency. This paper proposes a logic-guided data extraction framework combining LLM-based extraction with Answer Set Programming (ASP). The LLM produces candidate facts, whereas ASP performs validation, inference, consistency checking, and control. Unlike existing pipelines that query the LLM independently for all target predicates, the proposed approach uses ASP reasoning to identify which predicates are logically admissible at each stage and to guide extraction queries. By interleaving LLM calls with ASP derivation, the framework infers logically implied facts without further extraction and detects inconsistencies early. We formalize the pipeline and prove that, under mild assumptions, it is equivalent to the baseline approach with respect to the final extracted facts, while requiring fewer LLM calls. We also introduce a caching mechanism for logic-based control queries, exploiting monotonicity of conjunctive queries over incrementally constructed fact sets to reduce solver invocations. Experiments on ASP-derived benchmarks show that the framework reduces LLM calls and improves extraction quality by mitigating spurious outputs, demonstrating the value of non-monotonic logic programming for controlled semantic extraction.

AI.tldr: Logic-guided framework combining LLMs for semantic extraction with ASP for validation and control, reducing LLM calls while maintaining extraction quality.
AI.motivation: LLMs are unreliable for semantic data extraction requiring complex combinatorial reasoning and global consistency, needing a more controlled approach.
AI.method: Proposes interleaving LLM extraction with Answer Set Programming (ASP) reasoning: LLM produces candidate facts, ASP validates, checks consistency, infers implied facts, and guides which predicates to extract next.
AI.result: Framework reduces LLM calls while being equivalent to baseline in final extracted facts, with caching mechanism further reducing solver invocations. Experiments show improved extraction quality by mitigating spurious outputs.
AI.conclusion: Logic-guided approach combining LLMs with ASP provides an effective method for controlled semantic extraction, demonstrating value of non-monotonic logic programming for such tasks.

title: Cross-Subject Semantic Decoding with Shared-Space Alignment for Generalized Neural Representation Learning
summary: Generalizing across subjects remains challenging in invasive neural recordings because electrode configurations, anatomical structures, and neural signal patterns vary substantially across individuals. To investigate such inter-subject variability, we propose a cross-subject semantic decoding framework that aligns neural responses to speech perception from multiple subjects into a shared latent space and learns a mapping from the aligned neural representations to contextual embeddings. More specifically, using electrocorticography data collected during natural language comprehension, we estimate the shared space using the shared response model and train a decoder to predict contextual semantic embeddings from projected neural responses. For a held-out subject, we estimate a subject-specific projection into the predefined shared space, and directly apply the pretrained decoder without any retraining. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms baseline methods across evaluation settings and exhibits a reduced performance drop from source subject to held-out subject testing, indicating improved cross-subject generalization. These results suggest that aligning neural activity into a shared latent space, while decoding in a semantic embedding space, provides an effective strategy for improving cross-subject generalization by reducing subject-specific differences in neural responses while effectively capturing shared stimulus-related representations.

AI.tldr: A cross-subject neural decoding framework that aligns neural responses to speech perception into a shared latent space and decodes to semantic embeddings, enabling zero-shot generalization to new subjects without retraining.
AI.motivation: Generalizing across subjects is challenging in invasive neural recordings due to substantial variability in electrode configurations, anatomical structures, and neural signal patterns across individuals. There's a need for methods that can handle inter-subject variability in neural decoding tasks.
AI.method: Proposes a cross-subject semantic decoding framework that: 1) aligns neural responses to speech perception from multiple subjects into a shared latent space using the shared response model, 2) trains a decoder to predict contextual semantic embeddings from the aligned neural representations, 3) for held-out subjects, estimates a subject-specific projection into the predefined shared space and applies the pretrained decoder without retraining (zero-shot approach).
AI.result: The framework consistently outperforms baseline methods across evaluation settings and exhibits reduced performance drop from source subject to held-out subject testing, indicating improved cross-subject generalization. It reduces subject-specific differences while effectively capturing shared stimulus-related representations.
AI.conclusion: Aligning neural activity into a shared latent space while decoding in a semantic embedding space provides an effective strategy for improving cross-subject generalization by reducing subject-specific differences in neural responses while capturing shared stimulus-related representations.

title: Geometry-Guided Constraint Learning for LLM Safety Classification
summary: Safety as Polytope (SaP) learns linear half-space constraints in LLM hidden space but requires per-category tuning of the constraint count K. We show that sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature extraction resolves this: K=2 becomes optimal for 12/14 categories on Qwen3.5-9B, achieving 96-99% accuracy per category on our BeaverTails classification benchmark, largely eliminating the need for exhaustive sweeps (K=4-25 with random initialization). This convergence to two planes is consistent with the Linear Representation Hypothesis, providing suggestive evidence that safety boundaries in this setting admit a low-dimensional linear description in the SAE feature space. Building on this geometric perspective, we introduce a cone constraint whose learnable aperture adapts to each category's cluster concentration, stabilized by a three-phase training

AI.tldr: SAE feature extraction enables safety boundary learning with K=2 constraints instead of extensive tuning, showing safety boundaries are low-dimensional linear in SAE space, with new adaptive cone constraints.
AI.motivation: Safety as Polytope (SaP) requires per-category tuning of constraint count K, which is inefficient and time-consuming. The paper aims to simplify this process and better understand safety boundary geometry.
AI.method: Use sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature extraction to transform LLM hidden space, then learn linear half-space constraints. Show K=2 constraints become optimal with SAE features. Introduce cone constraint with learnable aperture adapted to each category's cluster concentration, stabilized by three-phase training.
AI.result: With SAE features, K=2 becomes optimal for 12/14 categories on Qwen3.5-9B, achieving 96-99% accuracy per category on BeaverTails benchmark. This largely eliminates need for exhaustive sweeps (previously K=4-25 with random initialization).
AI.conclusion: Safety boundaries in LLMs admit low-dimensional linear descriptions in SAE feature space (consistent with Linear Representation Hypothesis). The SAE approach provides a more efficient and geometrically interpretable safety boundary learning framework.

title: From Trajectories to Prefixes: Reusing Teacher Trajectories via Replayed Prefixes and Online Continuation
summary: Small language models are attractive backbones for interactive agents, but direct distillation from strong teacher trajectories often turns rich multi-turn behavior into one-shot imitation targets. This is inefficient in long-horizon environments, where early decisions shape later states and rewards. We propose Prefix-GRPO, a reinforcement learning framework that decomposes teacher trajectories into replay-aligned prefix queries and online continuations. Each prefix is replayed in the environment to recover a valid intermediate state, after which the student continues online interaction and receives task reward. Unlike response-only GRPO, Prefix-GRPO also applies clipped policy updates to historical assistant tokens inside the replayed prefix, using a policy-distilled SFT checkpoint to estimate their old log-probabilities. This unifies prefix learning and continuation learning within the same policy-optimization form. Experiments on TextCraft, BabyAI, and ALFWorld show that Prefix-GRPO improves small-model agents over distillation and standard RL baselines, while ablations show that replay alone is insufficient without explicit prefix-token optimization. The implementation and reproduction scripts are available at https://github.com/HappynessI/Prefix_GRPO.

AI.tldr: Prefix-GRPO: A reinforcement learning framework that decomposes teacher trajectories into prefix queries and online continuations to improve small language model agents through both prefix token optimization and continuation learning.
AI.motivation: Small language models are attractive for interactive agents, but direct distillation from teacher trajectories often reduces rich multi-turn behavior into inefficient one-shot imitation, which is problematic in long-horizon environments where early decisions affect later states and rewards.
AI.method: Prefix-GRPO decomposes teacher trajectories into replay-aligned prefix queries and online continuations. Each prefix is replayed to recover intermediate states, then the student continues online interaction for task rewards. It applies clipped policy updates to historical assistant tokens in replayed prefixes using policy-distilled SFT checkpoints to estimate old log-probabilities, unifying prefix learning and continuation learning.
AI.result: Experiments on TextCraft, BabyAI, and ALFWorld show Prefix-GRPO improves small-model agents over distillation and standard RL baselines. Ablations demonstrate that replay alone is insufficient without explicit prefix-token optimization.
AI.conclusion: Prefix-GRPO provides an effective reinforcement learning framework for training small language model agents that unifies prefix learning and continuation learning, outperforming existing methods and demonstrating the importance of explicit prefix-token optimization.

title: Rethinking Uncertainty Evaluation in Large Language Models
summary: Calibration is the primary criterion for evaluating LLM confidence, but it is insufficient: it admits trivially incoherent estimators, depends on the evaluation distribution, and does not test the extent to which the estimation can be interpreted as a consistent, underlying probability function. What we actually need is for LLM confidence estimates to satisfy the conditions required of coherent probabilistic beliefs. We formalize these conditions along three axes (structural coherence, faithfulness, and usefulness) and operationalize them as the C1 metrics. Widely used estimators systematically violate these conditions despite appearing well-calibrated: models assign lower confidence to logically easier questions 31\% of the time, and common interventions reducing RMSCE leave structural violations unchanged, suggesting that calibration is orthogonal to probabilistic validity. RLHF and chain-of-thought improve usefulness metrics without restoring coherence. Our results show current LLM confidence estimates cannot be interpreted as coherent probabilities; our framework provides the tools to measure and close this gap.

AI.tldr: Current LLM confidence calibration methods are insufficient as they allow trivially incoherent estimators and don't measure probabilistic coherence. The paper proposes a new framework (C1 metrics) to test three key conditions: structural coherence, faithfulness, and usefulness.
AI.motivation: Existing calibration metrics are inadequate because they admit trivially incoherent estimators, depend on evaluation distributions, and fail to test whether LLM confidence estimates can be interpreted as consistent probability functions. What's actually needed are coherent probabilistic beliefs.
AI.method: Formalize three key conditions for coherent probabilistic beliefs: structural coherence, faithfulness, and usefulness. Operationalize these as C1 metrics. Apply these metrics to evaluate widely used estimators and common calibration interventions.
AI.result: Widely used estimators systematically violate coherence conditions despite appearing well-calibrated. Models assign lower confidence to logically easier questions 31% of the time. Common calibration interventions reduce RMSCE but leave structural violations unchanged. RLHF and chain-of-thought improve usefulness metrics without restoring coherence.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM confidence estimates cannot be interpreted as coherent probabilities. The proposed framework provides tools to measure and address this gap, showing calibration is orthogonal to probabilistic validity.

title: Memory Merge DQN: Sensitivity Weighted Target Updates for Stable Value Learning
summary: Deep Q-networks use target networks to stabilise bootstrapped value learning, but the standard hard copy update also introduces a tradeoff. Holding the target network fixed, improves short term stability, yet each hard update abruptly replaces the target parameters with the newest online network and discards recent parameter history. This can produce sudden changes in the bootstrap target and may remove value function structure that remains useful later in training. This paper introduces Memory Merge DQN, a target network update mechanism that maintains a short memory of recent historical online network copies and constructs the target network by merging network parameters based on the Q-value sensitivity rather than copying only the newest online network. Memory Merge gives greater influence to parameters that remain locally important for current Q-value behaviour, while using a recency prior to keep the merged target close to the latest online parameters. The method is inspired by Fisher Weight Model Merging, but uses Q-value sensitivity rather than Fisher information as the weighting signal. This paper evaluates Memory Merge DQN on Atari environments against DQN, Averaged DQN, DQN with layer normalisation, and PQN (with gradient clipping). The results show that Memory Merge DQN is highly competitive and it achieves the largest number of first place final performance results among the evaluated methods, beats DQN, Averaged DQN, and PQN (with gradient clipping), and produces substantial gains in several games where preserving useful value-function parameters appears beneficial. These findings suggest that selectively merging recent parameter weights and history can improve the stability and final performance of DQN agents, and that target network design is an important mechanism for preserving useful value function structure during long horizon value learning.

AI.tldr: Memory Merge DQN improves DQN stability by merging recent historical network parameters based on Q-value sensitivity rather than hard copying the latest online network, achieving better performance on Atari games.
AI.motivation: Standard DQN hard copy updates cause abrupt target network changes that discard useful historical parameter structure, creating a tradeoff between stability and preserving valuable value function information.
AI.method: Memory Merge DQN maintains a short memory of recent online network copies and merges their parameters based on Q-value sensitivity (not Fisher information), giving weight to locally important parameters while using a recency prior to stay close to latest parameters.
AI.result: Memory Merge DQN achieves the largest number of first place final performance results on Atari environments, beating DQN, Averaged DQN, DQN with layer normalization, and PQN with gradient clipping, especially in games where preserving value-function parameters is beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Selective merging of recent parameter weights and history improves DQN stability and final performance, showing that target network design is crucial for preserving useful value function structure during long-horizon value learning.

title: Spectral-LSH: Sub-Quadratic Prompt Compression via Krylov-Projected Locality-Sensitive Hashing
summary:
Long-prompt inference remains expensive because prefill attention scales quadratically with sequence length. We propose Spectral-LSH, a training-free prompt compression method that operates before the prompt enters the language model. Spectral-LSH approximates the dominant components of an implicit attention-kernel operator using a Krylov subspace method together with random features, avoiding explicit $O(N^2)$ attention-kernel materialization. It then applies SimHash in the resulting attention eigenspace to group similar tokens and aggregate them into macro-tokens with causal positional assignments.
  We evaluate Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct on C4. Our experiments reveal a compression-ratio phase transition. Below $ρ= 4 \times$, local token redundancy is low enough that lightweight chunking typically provides the best latency--quality trade-off. Above $ρ= 8 \times$, the spectral path preserves quality that chunking loses. At $ρ= 16 \times$, Qwen2.5-7B (adaptive) reduces the PPL ratio from 353.409 to 196.963, while Qwen2.5-14B (adaptive) reduces it from 9.533 to 3.427.
  On a small long-context structured stress test containing JSON-like, code-like, and table-like inputs, local LSH also improves every metric over chunking at $8 \times$. The adaptive backend captures both regimes by using the chunk path at low compression and spectral clustering at high compression, although chunking remains the fastest backend in total latency.

AI.tldr: Spectral-LSH is a training-free prompt compression method that reduces quadratic attention costs in long-prompt inference by grouping similar tokens via spectral analysis and SimHash in attention eigenspace.
AI.motivation: Long-prompt inference is expensive due to quadratic scaling of prefill attention with sequence length, creating computational bottlenecks for processing long sequences.
AI.method: Uses Spectral-LSH to approximate dominant components of implicit attention-kernel operator via Krylov subspace method with random features, avoiding O(N²) attention-kernel materialization, then applies SimHash in attention eigenspace to group similar tokens into macro-tokens with causal positional assignments.
AI.result: Reveals compression-ratio phase transition: below 4× compression, chunking works best; above 8×, spectral method preserves quality better than chunking. At 16× compression, Qwen2.5-7B reduces PPL ratio from 353.409 to 196.963, Qwen2.5-14B from 9.533 to 3.427. Adaptive backend uses chunking at low compression and spectral clustering at high compression.
AI.conclusion: Spectral-LSH provides an effective training-free solution for prompt compression that outperforms chunking at high compression ratios, with an adaptive backend that automatically selects the optimal compression strategy based on compression ratio.

title: Leveraging Offline Supervision for Efficient and Generalizable Reinforcement Learning in Large-Scale Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: It is commonly observed that online reinforcement learning (RL) produces better-performing strategies than offline methods across a broad range of performance measures. In particular, RL-trained policies exhibit stronger out-of-distribution (OOD) behavior, where models trained only with imitation learning approaches often struggle. A recent study introduced an OOD-focused benchmark and reported that RL-trained vision-language-action (VLA) policies achieve noticeably better OOD performance and slightly better in-distribution (IND) performance than their counterparts trained with supervised fine-tuning (SFT). In this work, we investigate whether hybrid offline-online training can combine the advantages of both approaches. Specifically, we study RL methods regularized by offline supervision via either offline data or an offline-trained reference policy. We evaluate these approaches on the OOD benchmark and compare them with both offline-only training and standard RL. Our results show that although offline training achieves limited OOD performance by itself, incorporating offline supervision into RL preserves strong OOD capability while substantially improving training efficiency. In particular, the guided methods reach performance close to that of standard RL while requiring roughly half of the training budget. Rather than producing a trade-off between speed and OOD performance, the hybrid approach retains strong OOD capability while achieving this efficiency gain. Project page: https://alstar8.github.io/offline-supervision-vla-rl

AI.tldr: Hybrid offline-online RL training with offline supervision achieves similar performance to standard RL with roughly half the training budget while maintaining strong out-of-distribution capabilities.
AI.motivation: While RL-trained policies show better out-of-distribution (OOD) performance than offline methods, standard RL requires extensive training. The authors investigate whether combining offline supervision with online RL can maintain strong OOD performance while improving training efficiency.
AI.method: The paper studies RL methods regularized by offline supervision using either offline data or an offline-trained reference policy. These hybrid approaches are evaluated on an OOD benchmark and compared against both offline-only training and standard RL.
AI.result: Hybrid offline-online training preserves strong OOD capabilities while substantially improving training efficiency. Guided methods achieve performance close to standard RL with roughly half the training budget, without trading off OOD performance for speed.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating offline supervision into RL is an effective approach that maintains the strong OOD capabilities of RL while dramatically reducing training costs, offering a practical solution for training vision-language-action policies efficiently.

title: Beyond Tracking or Shortcut: Composition-Bounded Predictive States in Poker Autoregressive Models
summary: Hidden-state probes often recover latent labels in imperfect-information sequence models, but this alone does not establish that a model maintains a posterior belief distribution over hidden states. This paper studies this ambiguity in a no-range Limit Hold'em autoregressive model trained only on action and value targets, not on an opponent's hand or range. Opponent-range probes are positive after action/value controls in two of three seeds, and the behavior head predicts held-out actions about five percentage points above a baseline using only observable public history. However, visible public betting composition explains more opponent-range signal than residual hidden states, suggesting that most recoverable information comes from betting summaries. Action/value+composition baselines reach 16.5-16.7% top-10 accuracy while composition-residual hidden probes fall to 11.4-12.2%, and matched-composition comparisons are negative in every seed. We call this evidence pattern composition-bounded predictive support: hidden states remain behavior-predictive and opponent-range correlated, but most recoverable range information is explained by visible betting composition rather than residual hidden-state structure. This is a case-study claim about opponent-range representational evidence, not exact Bayesian posterior tracking or a causal belief mechanism. Synthetic control and oracle validations show that the same diagnostics accept posterior-sensitive states and reject raw composition states under matched controls. Thus positive belief probes should be interpreted through targeted alternatives before being treated as evidence of belief tracking.

AI.tldr: Hidden-state probes in poker AI can recover opponent-range information, but this doesn't prove the model maintains Bayesian posterior beliefs. Most recoverable range information comes from visible betting composition rather than residual hidden states.
AI.motivation: Hidden-state probes in sequence models can recover latent labels, but this alone doesn't establish that models maintain proper posterior belief distributions over hidden states. The paper investigates whether models trained only on action/value targets (without explicit opponent hand/range supervision) actually learn belief tracking or just exploit visible betting patterns.
AI.method: The study uses a no-limit Texas Hold'em autoregressive model trained only on action and value targets (not opponent's hand or range). It employs opponent-range probes with action/value controls, behavior head prediction from observable public history, and analyzes visible public betting composition vs. residual hidden states. Uses synthetic controls and oracle validations with matched-composition comparisons.
AI.result: 1. Opponent-range probes are positive after controls in 2 of 3 seeds. 2. Behavior head predicts held-out actions ~5% above baseline using only public history. 3. Visible betting composition explains more opponent-range signal than residual hidden states. 4. Action/value+composition baselines achieve 16.5-16.7% top-10 accuracy vs. 11.4-12.2% for composition-residual hidden probes. 5. Matched-composition comparisons are negative in every seed.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies "composition-bounded predictive support": hidden states remain predictive and opponent-range correlated, but most recoverable range information is explained by visible betting composition rather than residual hidden-state structure. Positive belief probes should be interpreted through targeted alternatives before being treated as evidence of belief tracking. This is a case-study about opponent-range representation, not exact Bayesian posterior tracking.

title: Predictive single cell foundation model for gene regulation and aging with privacy-preserving tabular learning
comment: 98 pages, 4 main figures, and 15 supplementary figures
summary: Pre-trained foundation models (FMs) have begun transforming single-cell genomics, but scaling them raises privacy concerns. Moreover, unlike text data, single-cell data is unordered and exhibits a unique tabular structure that current single-cell FMs overlook. We introduce Tabula, a privacy-preserving FM designed with federated learning (FL) that explicitly models the tabular structure of single-cell data. To deploy Tabula, we further developed Chiron, a decentralized AI agent-enabled platform for collaborative training across institutions without sharing raw data. Beyond strong performance across downstream benchmarks, Tabula reveals combinatorial regulatory logic across diverse biological systems, including hematopoiesis, pancreatic endogenesis, neurogenesis, and cardiogenesis. Using a new scRNA-seq dataset of paired young and aged human fibroblasts, Tabula nominates rejuvenation factors through age- and identity score-guided in silico prioritization, outperforming conventional approaches. Thus, Tabula represents an important advance in single-cell foundation modeling by integrating tabular learning with FL, paving the way toward privacy-preserving virtual cells for human health.

AI.tldr: Tabula is a privacy-preserving foundation model for single-cell genomics that uses federated learning to preserve data privacy and explicitly models the tabular structure of single-cell data, outperforming conventional approaches in various biological applications.
AI.motivation: Current single-cell foundation models have two key limitations: 1) They raise privacy concerns when scaling up, and 2) They overlook the unique tabular structure of single-cell data (which is unordered and has a specific tabular format unlike text data). There's a need for models that preserve privacy while properly capturing the structural characteristics of single-cell genomic data.
AI.method: The authors developed Tabula, a privacy-preserving foundation model that uses federated learning (FL) to enable collaborative training across institutions without sharing raw data. They also created Chiron, a decentralized AI agent-enabled platform to deploy Tabula. The model explicitly models the tabular structure of single-cell data rather than treating it like text data.
AI.result: Tabula demonstrates strong performance across downstream benchmarks and reveals combinatorial regulatory logic in diverse biological systems (hematopoiesis, pancreatic endogenesis, neurogenesis, cardiogenesis). Using a new scRNA-seq dataset of young and aged human fibroblasts, Tabula outperforms conventional approaches in nominating rejuvenation factors through age- and identity score-guided in silico prioritization.
AI.conclusion: Tabula represents a significant advance in single-cell foundation modeling by integrating tabular learning with federated learning, paving the way toward privacy-preserving virtual cells for human health applications.

title: Mitigating Scaffolding Collapse in Socratic Tutors via Representation Alignment
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based Socratic tutors increasingly guide students through multi-turn questioning, but they can suffer from scaffolding collapse: under sustained student pressure, a tutor gradually abandons guided inquiry and reveals solutions directly. Prior defenses primarily constrain observable responses through prompting, preference optimization, or filtering, leaving the internal representation drift that precedes trajectory-level collapse largely unaddressed. We propose Scaffold-Preserving Representation Alignment, a two-stage framework that first warms up a Socratic tutor with supervised fine-tuning, then combines trajectory-weighted direct preference optimization with a margin-preserving representation loss anchored to frozen reference states. Our method is designed to maintain separation between scaffold-preserving and collapse-inducing hidden states across dialogue turns. We evaluate our method across five STEM disciplines and five red-teaming attack strategies. On Qwen3-8B, our method lowers Collapse Rate to 32%, delays average collapse onset beyond nine turns, and keeps over-refusal low, suggesting that representation-level alignment can improve the robustness of long-horizon Socratic tutoring under our red-teaming protocol.

AI.tldr: A two-stage framework using representation alignment to prevent LLM Socratic tutors from abandoning guided inquiry under student pressure, reducing collapse rate to 32% and delaying collapse onset beyond 9 turns.
AI.motivation: LLM-based Socratic tutors suffer from "scaffolding collapse" where under sustained student pressure, they gradually abandon guided inquiry and directly reveal solutions. Prior defenses only constrain observable responses without addressing the internal representation drift that precedes collapse.
AI.method: Scaffold-Preserving Representation Alignment: a two-stage framework that 1) warms up Socratic tutor with supervised fine-tuning, then 2) combines trajectory-weighted direct preference optimization with margin-preserving representation loss anchored to frozen reference states to maintain separation between scaffold-preserving and collapse-inducing hidden states.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B model: lowers collapse rate to 32%, delays average collapse onset beyond nine turns, keeps over-refusal low. Evaluated across five STEM disciplines and five red-teaming attack strategies.
AI.conclusion: Representation-level alignment can improve the robustness of long-horizon Socratic tutoring under red-teaming protocols by addressing the internal representation drift that leads to scaffolding collapse.

title: When Does Consensus Beat Voting? A Critical Analysis of Statistical Label Fusion in Medical Image Segmentation
summary: This paper provides a rigorous, self-contained investigation of consensus segmentation. We derive the mathematical foundations from first principles -- the generative model, EM algorithm, Van Leemput's marginalization analysis, identifiability conditions, Spatial STAPLE, and deep variational formulations -- and validate each theoretical prediction through controlled experiments. The central finding is sobering: under common conditions, STAPLE reduces to thresholded majority voting, suffers 95% EM suboptimality, and collapses under class imbalance. These are not edge cases but typical scenarios in medical imaging. Majority voting -- simple, non-parametric, and robust -- is a surprisingly strong baseline that the field has perhaps too hastily dismissed in favor of more "sophisticated" methods. At the same time, the deep consensus model demonstrates that the consensus problem is not inherently difficult -- it becomes tractable when the image is used alongside the labels. And conformal prediction shows that formal uncertainty guarantees are achievable and practical. We hope this work encourages practitioners to critically evaluate their consensus methods rather than applying STAPLE by default, and provides the mathematical and empirical foundation for more principled approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a comprehensive mathematical analysis of consensus segmentation methods, revealing that STAPLE often reduces to majority voting, suffers from severe limitations, and that simpler methods like majority voting perform surprisingly well in practice.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to provide rigorous mathematical foundations for consensus segmentation methods to challenge the common practice of automatically applying STAPLE without critical evaluation. They seek to show that field may have dismissed simpler methods like majority voting too quickly in favor of more "sophisticated" approaches.
AI.method: The paper takes a theoretical approach by deriving mathematical foundations from first principles including generative models, EM algorithm, Van Leemput's marginalization analysis, identifiability conditions, Spatial STAPLE, and deep variational formulations. Each theoretical prediction is validated through controlled experiments. The analysis compares STAPLE, majority voting, deep consensus models, and conformal prediction approaches.
AI.result: Key findings: STAPLE reduces to thresholded majority voting under common conditions, suffers from 95% EM suboptimality, and collapses under class imbalance. Majority voting proves surprisingly robust and effective as a baseline. Deep consensus models show the problem becomes tractable when using images alongside labels. Conformal prediction demonstrates practical uncertainty guarantees are achievable.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that practitioners should critically evaluate consensus methods rather than defaulting to STAPLE, and provides mathematical foundations for more principled approaches. Simple, non-parametric methods like majority voting are often more robust than complex alternatives in medical imaging applications.

title: Euclean: Automated Geometry Problem Formalization with Unified Verification in Lean
summary: Recent formal reasoning systems have reached IMO-level performance, yet they leave a fragmented landscape: algebra and number theory are handled in Lean, while geometry still relies on domain-specific languages with limited formal guarantees. This split increases the trusted computing base and hinders unified model development. Existing geometry-in-Lean efforts (LeanEuclid, LeanGeo) introduce custom axiom systems incompatible with standard Mathlib, and their small scale ($<$ 1,100 problems) limits large-scale training. Native Mathlib autoformalization of geometry, however, poses distinct challenges: implicit diagrammatic assumptions (e.g., topological configuration and non-degeneracy) must be made explicit rather than deferred to external solvers, and models must adapt to Mathlib's small, rapidly evolving geometry infrastructure. We present Euclean, a four-stage framework - constraint explication, configuration anchoring, formalization mapping, and iterative repair - for automatically formalizing geometry in native Mathlib. We construct OMNI-Geometry (768 competition problems) and Numina-Geometry (177,597 problems), the largest geometry formalization dataset in Lean. Human evaluation shows 48.89% TOP1 and 73.33% TOP5 accuracy. Training Goedel v2 on our formalizations improves proof success from 13.6% to 15.1%, validating dataset quality for unified neural theorem proving. Code and datasets: https://github.com/tlb-22/Euclean.

AI.tldr: Euclean is a framework for autoformalizing geometry problems into Lean's Mathlib, addressing the gap between algebra/number theory and geometry formalization, enabling large-scale training datasets for unified neural theorem proving.
AI.motivation: Current formal reasoning systems are fragmented - algebra and number theory use Lean while geometry relies on domain-specific languages with limited formal guarantees. Existing geometry-in-Lean efforts have custom axiom systems incompatible with Mathlib and small datasets, hindering unified model development.
AI.method: Four-stage framework: 1) Constraint explication (making implicit diagrammatic assumptions explicit), 2) Configuration anchoring, 3) Formalization mapping, 4) Iterative repair. Used to create OMNI-Geometry (768 competition problems) and Numina-Geometry (177,597 problems) datasets.
AI.result: Created the largest geometry formalization dataset in Lean. Human evaluation shows 48.89% TOP1 and 73.33% TOP5 accuracy. Training Goedel v2 on these formalizations improves proof success from 13.6% to 15.1%.
AI.conclusion: Euclean successfully bridges the geometry formalization gap in Lean, providing high-quality datasets that improve neural theorem proving performance and enable unified formal reasoning across mathematical domains.

title: Recovering Clinical Utility Under Differential Privacy: Empirical Validation of Adaptive Federated Aggregation on Heterogeneous Cardiovascular Datasets
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures. Submitted to the Journal of the Brazilian Computer Society (JBCS)
summary: Validating federated learning frameworks on real clinical data is an essential step between proof-of-concept demonstrations in controlled synthetic environments and deployment in real multicenter healthcare settings. A prior architectural study by the same authors (Tertulino and Alencar, 2026) demonstrated, on a synthetic six-feature benchmark, that server-side adaptive optimization acts as a temporal denoiser for Differential Privacy noise, answering an open challenge identified in the original pipeline work (Tertulino, 2025). That study used synthetically generated data and explicitly identified real-world validation as a priority future direction. The present work addresses this gap by validating the FedCVR framework on five publicly available real cardiovascular datasets (Framingham, Cleveland, Hungarian, Switzerland, and Long Beach VA), harmonized to the 13-attribute UCI Heart Disease schema and configured as a heterogeneous federated scenario with leave-one-institution-out cross-validation. Results demonstrate that FedCVR preserves its adaptive advantage on real data, achieving an F1-Score of 79.2% and AUC of 0.96 under the operational privacy budget (noise multiplier = 0.8, privacy budget epsilon approximately 4.2), while statistically outperforming standard FedAvg on all evaluated metrics (paired t-tests, all p <= 0.003, significant under the Bonferroni-corrected threshold). The measured privacy cost on real data confirms the graceful degradation pattern observed in the synthetic experiments, providing empirical evidence of the framework's clinical viability in genuine multicenter contexts.

AI.tldr: FedCVR framework validates on real cardiovascular datasets, showing adaptive optimization preserves differential privacy advantages with clinical viability.
AI.motivation: Previous work on synthetic data showed adaptive optimization acts as temporal denoiser for differential privacy, but real-world validation was needed before clinical deployment.
AI.method: Validated FedCVR on five real cardiovascular datasets harmonized to 13-attribute schema, using leave-one-institution-out cross-validation in heterogeneous federated scenario with operational privacy parameters (noise multiplier = 0.8, epsilon ≈ 4.2).
AI.result: FedCVR achieved F1-Score of 79.2% and AUC of 0.96, statistically outperforming standard FedAvg on all metrics (all p ≤ 0.003). Privacy cost showed graceful degradation pattern confirming synthetic findings.
AI.conclusion: Framework preserves adaptive advantages on real clinical data, providing empirical evidence of clinical viability for genuine multicenter healthcare applications.

title: CrackedPDFs: A Controlled Benchmark for Hidden Prompt Injection in PDFs
summary: Document-based LLM systems often flatten a PDF before guardrails inspect it. That step can discard evidence that an instruction was never visible to the user. We introduce CrackedPDFs, a controlled benchmark for hidden prompt injection in PDFs. The benchmark contains 29,322 generated PDFs from 4,983 base docu ments. It includes 9,774 injected files and 19,548 benign or matched-confounder files. We evaluate PromptGuard and a rule baseline. We also evaluate structural only learned models and a sanitized hybrid detector. The evaluation uses held-out provenance splits and paired benign-confounder controls. It also uses label-shuffle checks and shortcut audits. On a 2,919-document held-out test set, the hybrid de tector reaches 0.960 F1. ROC-AUC is 0.998 and PR-AUC is 0.997. It also ranks injected files above matched benign confounders in 95.9% of 973 pairs. Prompt Guard has low recall when given extracted text only. Structural-only learned mod els are weak under paired controls. A text-only TF-IDF model reaches perfect held-out scores but fails shortcut audits. These results show that document-aware hybrid detection is useful under controlled paired evaluation. They do not show broad real-world robustness or reliable cross-family generalization.

AI.tldr: CrackedPDFs is a benchmark for hidden prompt injection attacks in PDFs, showing that hybrid document-aware detection works well in controlled settings but lacks real-world robustness.
AI.motivation: Document-based LLM systems often flatten PDFs before inspection, which can discard evidence that malicious instructions were never visible to users, creating a security vulnerability for hidden prompt injection attacks.
AI.method: Created CrackedPDFs benchmark with 29,322 generated PDFs (9,774 injected, 19,548 benign) from 4,983 base documents. Evaluated PromptGuard, rule baselines, structural-only models, and sanitized hybrid detectors using held-out provenance splits, paired benign-confounder controls, label-shuffle checks, and shortcut audits.
AI.result: Hybrid detector achieved 0.960 F1, 0.998 ROC-AUC, and 0.997 PR-AUC on 2,919-document test set, ranking injected files above matched benign confounders in 95.9% of 973 pairs. PromptGuard had low recall with extracted text only, structural-only models were weak, and TF-IDF model passed shortcuts audits despite perfect scores.
AI.conclusion: Document-aware hybrid detection is useful in controlled paired evaluation but lacks demonstrated real-world robustness or reliable cross-family generalization, highlighting the need for more comprehensive testing.

title: Structured Latent Space Modeling over Multi-Scale Temporal Patches for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multivariate time series encode structural patterns that unfold across multiple temporal scales, yet most forecasting backbones treat learned representations as transient byproducts of prediction, leaving the organizational geometry of these patterns underexploited. We introduce M2Patch, a CNN-based forecasting architecture that maps channel-independent multivariate observations into a structured latent space through two complementary differentiable constraints. Multi-scale patching decomposes the input into overlapping temporal granularities; depthwise separable convolutions with progressive dilation extract scale-specific features in linear time; and per-scale learned projections compress these features into a compact latent representation. The latent space is organized by an intra-scale smoothness constraint that enforces temporal continuity between adjacent patches, and an inter-scale alignment constraint, realized through learnable cross-scale mappings, that restores cross-granularity interaction within the channel-independent design, ensuring that all scales encode mutually consistent representations of the underlying dynamics. Experiments on ten real-world benchmarks show that M2Patch achieves 57 best and 34 second-best results across 40 forecasting settings, matching or exceeding representative baselines on most benchmarks while maintaining linear computational complexity and robustness to patch-level input corruption.

AI.tldr: M2Patch: A CNN-based multivariate time series forecasting model that uses multi-scale patching and structured latent space constraints to capture patterns across temporal scales efficiently with linear complexity.
AI.motivation: Most forecasting models treat learned representations as transient byproducts and fail to exploit the organizational geometry of multivariate time series patterns that unfold across multiple temporal scales, leaving this structural information underexplored.
AI.method: Uses multi-scale patching to decompose inputs into overlapping temporal granularities, depthwise separable convolutions with progressive dilation for scale-specific feature extraction, and learned projections for compact representation. The latent space is structured with intra-scale smoothness constraints (temporal continuity) and inter-scale alignment constraints (cross-granularity interaction) via learnable cross-scale mappings.
AI.result: Achieved 57 best and 34 second-best results across 40 forecasting settings on ten real-world benchmarks, matching or exceeding representative baselines while maintaining linear computational complexity and robustness to patch-level input corruption.
AI.conclusion: M2Patch effectively organizes multivariate time series patterns across temporal scales through structured latent constraints, achieving state-of-the-art forecasting performance with efficient linear computation.

title: HyGRL: Adaptive Hybrid Graph Reasoning for Multi-Entity Questions
summary: Multi-entity compositional questions pose significant challenges to existing retrieval-augmented language models. Conventional methods fall into a dilemma: standard RAG lacks dynamic reasoning, traditional Graph-RAG is limited by structural sparsity, and LLM-constructed Graph-RAG incurs prohibitive costs. We propose \textbf{\fwa}, a unified framework that embeds unstructured text into structured knowledge graphs, creating a heterogeneous network for flexible evidence retrieval. Reasoning is formulated as adaptive structure induction, learned via a robust two-stage process: (1) imitation learning distills heuristic expert signals, and (2) reinforcement learning refines the policy using LLM-driven preference rewards. Experiments demonstrate that {\fwa} effectively merges textual richness with structural knowledge, outperforming SOTA baselines in answer accuracy and reasoning fidelity while maintaining extremely low token costs and near real-time inference((code available at https://github.com/wjywjy123/HyGRL) .

AI.tldr: A new framework called \fwa (likely HyGRL) that embeds unstructured text into knowledge graphs for multi-entity compositional questions, using adaptive structure induction learned via imitation and reinforcement learning to combine textual richness with structural knowledge efficiently.
AI.motivation: Existing retrieval-augmented language models struggle with multi-entity compositional questions. Standard RAG lacks dynamic reasoning, traditional Graph-RAG suffers from structural sparsity, and LLM-constructed Graph-RAG is prohibitively expensive.
AI.method: Proposes \fwa framework that embeds unstructured text into structured knowledge graphs to create heterogeneous networks. Uses adaptive structure induction learned through a two-stage process: 1) imitation learning to distill heuristic expert signals, and 2) reinforcement learning to refine the policy using LLM-driven preference rewards.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in answer accuracy and reasoning fidelity while maintaining extremely low token costs and near real-time inference. Code is available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: \fwa effectively merges textual richness with structural knowledge, providing an efficient solution for multi-entity compositional question answering that addresses limitations of existing RAG approaches.

title: Reproducing Recurrent Transformers: The CoTFormer
comment: 7 pages, 3 figures, reproducibility report submitted to University of Southampton
summary: The CoTFormer architecture formalizes Chain-of-Thought as a form of recurrent latent computation, preserving intermediate states as attendable representations to mimic explicit reasoning traces. In this work, we evaluate CoTFormer and its structural variants across perplexity and compute efficiency metrics. Furthermore, we extend evaluation to controlled algorithmic settings to determine whether this recurrent framework improves out-of-distribution generalisation on inductive reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: CoTFormer formalizes Chain-of-Thought as recurrent latent computation, with evaluation showing its performance on perplexity, compute efficiency, and out-of-distribution generalization in algorithmic tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to formalize Chain-of-Thought reasoning as a recurrent latent computation framework and systematically evaluate its effectiveness compared to structural variants.
AI.method: Proposes CoTFormer architecture that preserves intermediate states as attendable representations to mimic explicit reasoning traces, with evaluation across perplexity and compute efficiency metrics, plus controlled algorithmic settings.
AI.result: The evaluation examines CoTFormer's performance on standard metrics and specifically tests whether the recurrent framework improves out-of-distribution generalization on inductive reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents an architectural formalization of Chain-of-Thought reasoning and provides systematic evaluation to determine its effectiveness for improving generalization in reasoning tasks.

title: ITPEval: Benchmarking Formal Translation Across Interactive Theorem Provers
summary: Formal theorem proving has emerged as a frontier challenge for machine learning, yet the ecosystem is fragmented: proofs remain siloed across incompatible systems, limiting both training data for learning-based provers and the portability of verified results. We present ITPEval, the first benchmark for evaluating automated formal proof translation across four major ITPs (Lean 4, Rocq, Isabelle, and HOL Light), spanning two distinct logical foundations.
