AI.method: Combines high-fidelity synthetic EHR generation using latent autoencoder-diffusion model with synthetic-pretrained FL. Includes heterogeneity-aware optimization with class-balanced local objectives, proximal regularization, adaptive server aggregation, post-hoc calibration, and federated-safe explainability (SHAP).
AI.result: Synthetic generator preserves data structure while protecting privacy; generated data shows strong utility. SynPre-FL improves robustness and scalability across 5-15 heterogeneous clients, especially under severe non-IID fragmentation. Calibration improves reliability, SHAP provides stable clinical feature attributions.
AI.conclusion: SynPre-FL provides a practical framework combining synthetic data with FL for privacy-aware, interpretable, and robust clinical prediction from distributed EHR data.

title: SenWorld: A Digital-Twin Simulation for Generating Context-Rich Evaluation Data
summary: Smartphone personal assistants reason over longitudinal personal data, yet evaluating them requires context-rich evaluation data whose correct answers are known, and real device traces are too privacy-sensitive to share. To address this challenge, we present SenWorld, a physically grounded, deterministic, event-sourced digital-twin simulation that generates such data with ground truth fixed by construction. In SenWorld, personas live through a full day in a world built from real map, weather, holiday, and network data; every observable signal is archived in full-system snapshots; and each evaluation case is labeled by a pointer to an existing record rather than by post-hoc annotation or a large language model (LLM) judge. We evaluate this method with 16 personas in Beijing. The generated data closely matches the held-out real-user benchmark in category distribution (Jensen--Shannon divergence (JSD) 0.070) and in the daily rhythm of communication records (JSD below 0.1), though generated records remain shorter than real ones. Without scripted interaction, personas form a fully reciprocated dialogue subgraph and differentiated behavioral repertoires. Projected into 717 evaluation cases, the generated data exposes 78 failures in a production smartphone assistant, concentrating on call and Short Message Service (SMS) records while contacts, schedules, and alarms never fail. The snapshot pointer confirms each failure as an assistant-side retrieval error, with no LLM judge involved. Overall, SenWorld offers a privacy-safe, reproducible, and distribution-checked path to evaluation data whose labels are fixed by construction.

AI.tldr: SenWorld is a digital-twin simulation that generates privacy-safe evaluation data for smartphone personal assistants, using real-world data and pointer-based labeling instead of LLM judges or post-hoc annotation.
AI.motivation: Smartphone personal assistants need context-rich evaluation data, but real device traces are too privacy-sensitive to share, and existing evaluation methods lack ground truth.
AI.method: SenWorld creates physically-grounded, deterministic digital twins that simulate personas living through full days using real map, weather, holiday, and network data, with all observable signals archived in full-system snapshots and evaluation cases labeled by pointers to existing records.
AI.result: Generated data closely matches real-user benchmarks (JSD 0.070), personas form natural interaction patterns, and testing on 16 personas in Beijing exposed 78 failures in a production smartphone assistant, mainly in call/SMS handling, with all failures confirmed as retrieval errors via snapshot pointers.
AI.conclusion: SenWorld provides a privacy-safe, reproducible, and distribution-checked approach to generating evaluation data with labels fixed by construction, enabling rigorous testing of smartphone assistants without privacy concerns or unreliable LLM judges.

title: The C-index illusion: discrimination without calibration in published survival models
summary: "Stop Chasing the C-index when Evaluating Survival Analysis Models" (ICML 2026, Spotlight) argued normatively, on synthetic data, that evaluating survival models by discrimination alone, i.e. the concordance index, produces systematically misleading model comparisons, because the metric ignores calibration and time-dependent accuracy. Whether this matters for real, published, non-clinical models has not been tested. We reproduce three published survival-ML models across three structurally distinct domains (hard-drive failure, peer-to-peer credit default, and user disengagement on digital platforms), validate our evaluation instrument against the anchor paper's own synthetic experiment, and test five pre-registered hypotheses under a Holm-corrected family-wise error rate. Three of five reject. A model that reproduces the published discrimination almost exactly (C = 0.9595 vs. 0.958 reported) fails a formal calibration test at p = 2.6e-136; a broad feature-ablation search finds no single attribute responsible for this discrimination, so the calibration failure is not an artifact of a trivial shortcut. A lender's estimated default risk is biased upward by roughly two percentage points, growing to nearly four points in the riskiest segment, when loan prepayment is treated as non-informative censoring rather than as a competing risk. A platform's churn model shows probability estimates that degrade with the prediction horizon even as its global discrimination stays within the pre-registered C-index band. A direct test of whether metric choice inverts which model is preferred does not reject, though with limited power given only two to three models per domain; the failure mode we document is better characterized as misplaced confidence in a chosen model than as choice of the wrong one. We release a reusable, pre-registered evaluation harness with full code and a single annotated notebook.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that evaluating survival analysis models using only the concordance index (C-index) is misleading, as it ignores calibration and time-dependent accuracy, leading to misplaced confidence in models that appear discriminative but have systematic biases in real-world applications across three domains.
AI.motivation: Previous work argued that using discrimination alone (C-index) for evaluating survival models is misleading, but this hadn't been tested on real published non-clinical models. The authors wanted to validate whether this theoretical concern actually matters in practice across different domains.
AI.method: The researchers reproduced three published survival-ML models across three distinct domains (hard-drive failure, peer-to-peer credit default, and user disengagement), validated their evaluation instrument against the anchor paper's synthetic experiment, and tested five pre-registered hypotheses with Holm-corrected family-wise error rate control. They performed feature-ablation searches and formal calibration tests.
AI.result: Three of five hypotheses were rejected: 1) A model reproducing published discrimination (C=0.9595 vs 0.958) failed calibration test at p=2.6e-136, 2) Default risk estimates were biased upward when treating prepayment as non-informative censoring vs competing risk, and 3) Probability estimates degraded with prediction horizon despite stable C-index. The failure mode is characterized as misplaced confidence rather than wrong model choice.
AI.conclusion: The study provides empirical evidence that relying solely on the C-index for survival model evaluation leads to systematic issues in real applications. Researchers should consider calibration and time-dependent accuracy alongside discrimination. The authors release a reusable, pre-registered evaluation harness to facilitate more comprehensive model assessment.

title: EvoThink: Evolving Thinking in Large Reasoning Models via Self-Pruning and Aha-Moment Preference Optimization
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) often suffer from overthinking due to redundant verification steps. Existing approaches for mitigating overthinking, such as fast-slow thinking switching and reasoning trajectory compression, fail to make a fine-grained distinction between beneficial and redundant steps within the LRM's reasoning process, and may thus impair reasoning capability in their pursuit of efficiency. To simultaneously improve reasoning efficiency and capability, we propose EvoThink, a framework that reduces redundant verification and encourages the exploration of new reasoning paths. EvoThink comprises two key components: Self-Pruning Training (SPT), an unsupervised method that iteratively prunes redundant reasoning steps and self-trains on the concise trajectories; and Aha-Moment Preference Optimization (AMPO), which, inspired by genetic algorithms, identifies valuable failed reasoning attempts, synthesizes from-wrong-to-right aha-moment data, and optimizes the model to internalize this reasoning pattern. Extensive evaluations across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks demonstrate that EvoThink not only substantially reduces inference-time token usage but also improves the reasoning capability of LRMs.

AI.tldr: EvoThink is a framework that reduces overthinking in Large Reasoning Models by pruning redundant verification steps and promoting exploration of new reasoning paths, improving both efficiency and reasoning capability.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models suffer from overthinking due to redundant verification steps. Existing approaches for mitigating overthinking make coarse distinctions between beneficial and redundant steps, potentially impairing reasoning capability while pursuing efficiency.
AI.method: EvoThink comprises two components: 1) Self-Pruning Training (SPT) - an unsupervised method that iteratively prunes redundant reasoning steps and self-trains on concise trajectories; 2) Aha-Moment Preference Optimization (AMPO) - inspired by genetic algorithms, identifies valuable failed reasoning attempts, synthesizes from-wrong-to-right aha-moment data, and optimizes the model to internalize this reasoning pattern.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks demonstrate that EvoThink substantially reduces inference-time token usage while improving the reasoning capability of Large Reasoning Models.
AI.conclusion: EvoThink simultaneously improves reasoning efficiency and capability by reducing redundant verification and encouraging exploration of new reasoning paths, offering a more sophisticated approach to addressing overthinking in Large Reasoning Models compared to existing methods.

title: Trustworthy Privacy-Preserving Multimodal Federated Learning for Personalised Breast Cancer Prediction
summary: Federated learning has emerged as a potential solution to privacy concerns associated with using sensitive health data for training predictive models, particularly in personalised cancer care. This research investigates whether federated learning can support the development of robust models for predicting tumour progression in breast cancer patients while addressing four critical deployment pillars: transparency, scalability, security, and fairness. This study evaluates a federated learning framework using multimodal data, including clinical information, tumour characteristics, biomarker data, and patient demographics, alongside medical imaging data such as MRI scans, to model changes in tumour characteristics over time. The performance of the federated approach was compared with that of a centralised model trained on aggregated data. The report then further examines strategies to enhance secure model updates, maintain performance across patient subgroups, and support scalability across institutions. The findings assess whether federated learning can achieve predictive performance comparable to centralised learning while preserving data locality. These results contribute to understanding the feasibility of privacy-preserving, multimodal predictive modelling and support future applications such as digital twins to assist clinicians and patients in personalised treatment planning.

AI.tldr: Federated learning with multimodal data can achieve comparable performance to centralized models for predicting breast cancer tumor progression while maintaining data privacy and addressing deployment challenges of transparency, scalability, security, and fairness.
AI.motivation: Privacy concerns with sensitive health data for personalized cancer care models, need to preserve patient data privacy while developing robust predictive models for tumor progression.
AI.method: Evaluated federated learning framework using multimodal data (clinical information, tumor characteristics, biomarkers, demographics, MRI scans) to model tumor changes over time, compared with centralized approach on aggregated data.
AI.result: Federated learning achieved predictive performance comparable to centralized learning while preserving data locality. Examined strategies for secure model updates, maintaining performance across patient subgroups, and supporting institutional scalability.
AI.conclusion: Federated learning is feasible for privacy-preserving, multimodal predictive modeling in cancer care, with potential applications for digital twins and personalized treatment planning while addressing critical deployment pillars.

title: The Giant Hippocampus: From Structural Monoculture to a System of Systems
summary: AI researchers describe state-of-the-art models as one thing repeated at scale: the Transformer, wired identically for text, pixels, or speech. Neuroscientists describe the cortex as a mosaic - dense Layer 4 in visual cortex for spatial encoding, thick Layers 5/6 in motion cortex for temporal integration - different jobs solved by different structures. This paper argues the gap is a structural error, not a stylistic one, and is measurable. A century of cytoarchitecture, from Brodmann to single-cell Patch-seq, shows distinct cognitive functions are implemented by qualitatively different structures, not by rescaling one template. The convolutional neural network is the field's own proof: local receptive fields and hierarchical depth encoded this prior directly, reaching strong image recognition on far less data than later architectures needed. The paper traces how this lesson was discarded: the "Hardware Lottery" made the Transformer the path of least resistance, not the principled choice, and Mixture-of-Experts, often cited as diversity, in fact partitions parameters among identical experts. A functionalist analysis shows the Transformer is best understood as a functional analog of the hippocampal formation, not a general-purpose cortex - the same mistake as treating cortex as one giant Broca's area, except the field has now standardized on a giant hippocampus, applied to tasks it was never built for: audition, executive gating, working memory. The paper closes with an alternative: a Heterogeneous Topological Network, a System of Systems in which distinct modules keep the inductive bias their computation demands and communicate through standardized interfaces. This is a design discipline for AI architects, not cognitive science: specify modularity before training, using structural evidence as a design input rather than reverse-engineering architecture from a trained model's behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI's focus on scaling identical Transformer architectures is fundamentally misguided, drawing from neuroscience evidence that different cognitive functions require distinct structural designs, not just scaled versions of one template.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies a fundamental gap between AI practice (scaling identical architectures like Transformers) and neuroscience understanding (cortex as a mosaic of specialized structures). This is seen as a structural error rather than just a stylistic difference, with measurable consequences for AI performance and efficiency.
AI.method: The paper uses a functionalist analysis combining evidence from a century of neuroscience (cytoarchitecture from Brodmann to Patch-seq), historical analysis of AI architectures, and critique of current practices like Mixture-of-Experts. It examines how the field discarded the lesson of specialized structures like CNNs in favor of Transformers due to the "Hardware Lottery."
AI.result: The analysis shows that Transformers function analogously to the hippocampal formation rather than general-purpose cortex, making them ill-suited for many tasks they're applied to (audition, executive gating, working memory). Mixture-of-Experts is shown to be superficial diversity that still uses identical components.
AI.conclusion: The paper proposes a Heterogeneous Topological Network framework as an alternative - a System of Systems where distinct modules maintain their required inductive biases and communicate through standardized interfaces. This represents a design discipline for AI architects that specifies modularity before training based on structural evidence.

title: Agent-Centric Animal Pose Forecasting
summary: Understanding animal behavior at an algorithmic level -- what animals attend to, how they form internal models and plans, and how this maps to action -- remains a central challenge in neuroscience and ethology. Data-driven generative models offer a path toward this understanding. We introduce a framework for training agent-centric autoregressive models of animal behavior from tracked pose, applicable to single animals and to groups in which each agent senses and responds to its conspecifics. Our models input egocentric sensory observations and output egocentric movements, mirroring the biological constraint that animals observe and act on the world from their own reference frame. Social behavior emerges from agents independently sensing and responding to one another. This agent-centric formulation requires managing many parallel representations of the same data, along with ML-specific transformations like discretization. We release a general-purpose library focused on the composable sequences of operations that translate between these representations. We show that trained models capture the distribution of social behavior in groups of courting Drosophila, and our library includes quantitative tools for measuring fit. We demonstrate how the library supports systematic comparison across input and output representations and that it adapts straightforwardly to a new domain.

AI.tldr: A framework for training agent-centric autoregressive models of animal behavior from pose tracking data, applicable to single animals or social groups, with a released general-purpose library for managing parallel representations.
AI.motivation: Understanding animal behavior at an algorithmic level - what animals attend to, how they form internal models and plans, and how this maps to action - remains a central challenge in neuroscience and ethology. Data-driven generative models offer a path toward this understanding.
AI.method: Introduces a framework for training agent-centric autoregressive models of animal behavior from tracked pose data. The models input egocentric sensory observations and output egocentric movements, mirroring biological constraints. Social behavior emerges from agents independently sensing and responding to one another. Includes a general-purpose library focused on composable sequences of operations for translating between different representations.
AI.result: Trained models capture the distribution of social behavior in groups of courting Drosophila. The library includes quantitative tools for measuring fit and supports systematic comparison across input and output representations. The approach adapts straightforwardly to new domains.
AI.conclusion: The agent-centric autoregressive framework and accompanying library provide an effective approach for modeling animal behavior from tracked pose data, enabling better understanding of animal cognition and social interactions through data-driven generative modeling.

title: Coordinating from Memory: Graph-Structured Experience Reuse for Multi-Agent Adaptation in Dynamic Manufacturing
summary: Dynamic manufacturing environments require multi-agent systems to coordinate effectively under frequent operational disturbances such as machine failures, urgent job arrivals, and processing time variations. Existing multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches treat each disturbance episode independently, discarding valuable coordination experience that could accelerate future adaptation. In this paper, we propose a Graph-Structured Experiential Memory (GSEM) framework for multi-agent coordination in dynamic manufacturing. The framework encodes historical coordination episodes as heterogeneous relational graphs that capture task dependencies, machine states, and inter-agent collaboration patterns. When a new disturbance occurs, a graph neural network-based retrieval mechanism identifies structurally similar past episodes, enabling experience-guided policy adaptation rather than learning from scratch. Experiments on dynamic flexible job-shop scheduling benchmarks with three disturbance types show that GSEM reduces makespan by 4.1%-10.0% and adaptation time by 33%-38% compared to the strongest memory-augmented baseline, with the advantage increasing under higher disturbance frequency. Ablation studies and cross-disturbance transfer experiments further validate the necessity of graph-structured encoding and similarity-based retrieval and demonstrate the cross-disturbance generalizability of learned coordination patterns.

AI.tldr: GSEM is a graph-structured experiential memory framework that enables multi-agent systems in manufacturing to reuse past coordination experiences to adapt faster to operational disturbances, reducing both makespan and adaptation time.
AI.motivation: Dynamic manufacturing environments face frequent operational disturbances (machine failures, urgent job arrivals, processing time variations) requiring effective multi-agent coordination. Existing multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches discard valuable coordination experience from past disturbances, forcing agents to learn from scratch for each new disturbance episode.
AI.method: Propose Graph-Structured Experiential Memory (GSEM) framework that encodes historical coordination episodes as heterogeneous relational graphs capturing task dependencies, machine states, and inter-agent collaboration patterns. When a new disturbance occurs, a graph neural network-based retrieval mechanism identifies structurally similar past episodes, enabling experience-guided policy adaptation rather than learning from scratch.
AI.result: Experiments on dynamic flexible job-shop scheduling benchmarks with three disturbance types show GSEM reduces makespan by 4.1%-10.0% and adaptation time by 33%-38% compared to the strongest memory-augmented baseline. The advantage increases under higher disturbance frequency. Ablation studies and cross-disturbance transfer experiments validate the necessity of graph-structured encoding and similarity-based retrieval, demonstrating cross-disturbance generalizability of learned coordination patterns.
AI.conclusion: The GSEM framework successfully enables multi-agent systems in manufacturing to leverage past coordination experiences through graph-structured memory and similarity-based retrieval, significantly improving both adaptation speed and solution quality in dynamic manufacturing environments with frequent disturbances.

title: End-to-End Differential Privacy in Training Deep Neural Network Classifiers
summary: Differentially private machine learning enables model training on sensitive data while ensuring that individual data is unlikely to be recoverable from the parameters of the resulting model. However, existing work often privatizes both training inputs and their labels, and these protections may be conservative when labels are public or can be safely made public. Therefore, in this work we propose a novel private training framework that instead privatizes training inputs while keeping labels public. We consider neural networks with softmax output layers, and thus the mapping from training inputs to the output of the softmax layer is a mapping onto the unit simplex. We randomize softmax outputs during training by applying the Dirichlet mechanism to enforce differential privacy for the training inputs, hence the ``end-to-end'' label. Because training data is reused across multiple training epochs, we use the notion of \Renyi differential privacy to formulate tight bounds on the strength of privacy provided by the Dirichlet mechanism across repeated uses. We show empirically that we attain new state-of-the-art accuracy when training from scratch on CIFAR10, MNIST, MedMNIST, FashionMNIST, and SVHN across all privacy budgets evaluated. Notably, when implementing $(ε, δ)$-differential privacy with $δ=10^{-5}$, we improve the prior state-of-the-art accuracy from $78.37\%$ to $88.17\%$ at $ε=4$ on CIFAR10, and our approach has $82.96\%$ accuracy even for $ε=1$, which significantly outperforms prior work.

AI.tldr: A novel differentially private training framework that privatizes only training inputs (not labels) using a Dirichlet mechanism on softmax outputs, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on multiple datasets while maintaining privacy.
AI.motivation: Existing differential privacy methods often privatize both training inputs and labels, which can be overly conservative when labels are public or can safely be made public. This leads to unnecessary accuracy loss, so the authors propose a more efficient approach that only privatizes inputs while keeping labels public.
AI.method: The framework uses neural networks with softmax output layers. The key innovation is applying the Dirichlet mechanism to randomize softmax outputs during training, enforcing differential privacy for training inputs only. The authors use Renyi differential privacy to formulate tight privacy bounds for repeated data usage across training epochs.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art accuracy when training from scratch on CIFAR10, MNIST, MedMNIST, FashionMNIST, and SVHN across all privacy budgets evaluated. Specifically for (ε, δ)-DP with δ=10⁻⁵, improves prior SOTA accuracy from 78.37% to 88.17% at ε=4 on CIFAR10, and achieves 82.96% accuracy even at ε=1, significantly outperforming prior work.
AI.conclusion: The proposed input-only privatization approach with Dirichlet mechanism and Renyi DP provides a more efficient privacy-preserving training framework that significantly improves accuracy while maintaining strong privacy guarantees, especially when labels can be safely made public.

title: CLARK: Closed-loop Learning for Adaptive Reasoning over Knowledge Graphs
comment: 18 pages, 3 Figures, 1 algorithm, 3 tables, submitted to The International Semantic Web Conference'26
summary: Machine Learning models are widely used for automating classification tasks by extracting statistical patterns from data. However, their performance deteriorates if the data distribution changes, making them ill-suited to handle uncertain and evolving information. Moreover, they provide limited support for integrating prior knowledge. To address these limitations, we present CLARK (Closed-loop Learning for Adaptive Reasoning over Knowledge Graphs), a framework that integrates knowledge graphs, symbolic rule mining, and probabilistic reasoning under the Logic Programs with Markov Logic Networks (LP$^{\text{MLN}}$) formalism. Starting from CACTUS-derived KGs, CLARK translates graph structure into an LP$^{\text{MLN}}$ program and iteratively enriches it with candidate rules proposed by symbolic learners. These rules are calibrated through probabilistic weight learning, enabling reasoning under uncertainty and refinement of the underlying graph structure. We evaluate CLARK on two medical datasets, analysing both rule quality and downstream classification performance. Results demonstrate that CLARK leads to improved classification performance and more generalisable inference. Overall, CLARK provides a principled approach to constructing adaptive, interpretable, knowledge-driven models for classification.

AI.tldr: CLARK is a framework combining knowledge graphs, symbolic rule mining, and probabilistic reasoning to create adaptive, interpretable classification models that handle uncertainty and evolving data distributions.
AI.motivation: Traditional ML models struggle with changing data distributions and limited ability to integrate prior knowledge, making them unsuitable for uncertain, evolving information domains.
AI.method: Integrates knowledge graphs, symbolic rule mining, and probabilistic reasoning using Logic Programs with Markov Logic Networks (LP^MLN). Translates KG structure into LP^MLN programs, iteratively enriches with candidate rules from symbolic learners, and calibrates rules through probabilistic weight learning.
AI.result: Evaluation on two medical datasets shows improved classification performance and more generalizable inference compared to traditional approaches.
AI.conclusion: CLARK provides a principled approach to construct adaptive, interpretable, knowledge-driven models that can handle uncertainty and evolving information better than conventional ML methods.

title: The Mechanism Matters: When Knowledge Graphs Help Reinforcement Learning
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) are widely used to inject prior knowledge into reinforcement learning (RL), yet the literature is dominated by single-domain, positive-result method papers, so we lack a systematic account of when KG structure helps an agent, when it is neutral, and when it hurts. We conduct a controlled study that independently varies the RL task, the injection mechanism (state features, action masking, or potential-based reward shaping), and KG quality. Using a synthetic, fully controllable KG over MiniGrid environments, we report three findings. First, on compositional sparse-reward tasks structured KG guidance improves sample efficiency and solve reliability (70% to 97% of seeds), and a shuffle control that permutes the KG's edges while preserving their count collapses the benefit toward baseline (masking p=0.0001; shaping p=0.006), so the gain is structural rather than generic regularization. Second, KG value scales with the amount of task-relevant knowledge the graph contains. Third, and most consequential, safety depends on the mechanism: soft, optimality-preserving injection benefits from correct knowledge and harmlessly ignores incorrect knowledge, whereas hard masking is brittle, forbidding essential actions when the KG is incomplete or corrupted and making a wrong KG worse than none. A UMLS-derived clinical case study on sepsis management under offline RL is a careful null, underscoring that benefits require task structure the chosen mechanism can exploit. Our results give practitioners concrete guidance on how, and how much, to trust a KG when using it to guide RL.

AI.tldr: Knowledge graphs can improve RL sample efficiency and reliability in structured tasks, but benefits depend on KG quality and injection mechanism - soft methods are robust while hard masking can be harmful with imperfect KGs.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of systematic understanding about when knowledge graph structure helps, harms, or is neutral in reinforcement learning, despite widespread use of KGs to inject prior knowledge into RL systems.
AI.method: Conducted controlled study varying RL task, KG injection mechanism (state features, action masking, reward shaping), and KG quality using synthetic, controllable KGs over MiniGrid environments, plus a clinical sepsis management case study with UMLS-derived KG.
AI.result: 1) Structured KG guidance improves sample efficiency and solve reliability (70% to 97% success) in compositional sparse-reward tasks; 2) KG value scales with task-relevant knowledge; 3) Safety depends on mechanism: soft injection benefits from correct knowledge and ignores incorrect knowledge, while hard masking is brittle and can be harmful with incomplete/corrupted KGs.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides practical guidance for RL practitioners: benefits require task structure that mechanisms can exploit, soft injection methods are optimality-preserving and robust, while hard masking requires careful trust in KG completeness and correctness.

title: Safe Remediation as Risk-Constrained Intervention Decision in Microservice Systems
summary: In modern IT operations (IT-Ops), the cost of an incorrect repair often exceeds the cost of no action at all. Yet existing automated remediation systems are designed to generate actions rather than to decide whether intervention is warranted, leaving safety as an afterthought enforced by manual approval. This paper makes three contributions to close this gap: (i) we reformulate safe remediation as a risk-constrained intervention decision problem and cast it as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP), in which the agent maximizes repair success subject to a bounded false remediation rate (FRR); (ii) we introduce a three-dimensional risk decomposition comprising blast radius, reversibility, and epistemic uncertainty, providing operators with an interpretable per-action safety interface; and (iii) we design a context-adaptive human-in-the-loop (HITL) gate that turns escalation from a binary failsafe into a bandwidth-aware control layer responsive to on-call load and business criticality. The full policy is learned offline from historical incident logs, enabling explicit control of the expected FRR. Experiments on the Train Ticket microservice benchmark with Chaos Mesh fault injection and an RCAEval-aligned fault taxonomy show that our framework reduces FRR by 39% while improving repair success by 2.5 points over a strong runbook baseline, and reduces on-call escalation load by 17% relative to a fixed-threshold variant.

AI.tldr: A safe automated remediation framework that decides when to intervene in IT operations using risk-constrained decision making with interpretable safety interfaces, reducing false remediation rates by 39% while maintaining repair success.
AI.motivation: Current automated remediation systems in IT operations focus on generating actions rather than deciding whether intervention is warranted, with safety often addressed manually. Incorrect repairs can be more costly than taking no action, creating a need for systems that explicitly balance repair success with safety.
AI.method: 1) Formulates safe remediation as a risk-constrained intervention decision problem using Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) to maximize repair success while bounding false remediation rate. 2) Introduces three-dimensional risk decomposition (blast radius, reversibility, epistemic uncertainty) for interpretable safety. 3) Designs context-adaptive human-in-the-loop gate that considers on-call load and business criticality.
AI.result: On the Train Ticket microservice benchmark with Chaos Mesh fault injection: reduces false remediation rate (FRR) by 39%, improves repair success by 2.5 percentage points over baseline, and reduces on-call escalation load by 17% compared to fixed-threshold approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the safety gap in automated IT remediation by providing explicit control over intervention decisions with interpretable safety interfaces, demonstrating significant improvements in both safety and operational efficiency compared to existing approaches.

title: SCPP: A Unified Python Library for Soft Clustering
summary: In this paper, we present SCPP (Soft Clustering Python Package), an open-source Python framework for soft clustering. SCPP establishes a canonical, scikit-learn-compatible estimator interface that standardizes model training, prediction, membership representation, evaluation, and benchmarking across heterogeneous soft clustering methods, including fuzzy, probabilistic, graph-based, matrix factorization, and deep learning methods. The framework currently integrates 40 representative algorithms together with a comprehensive benchmarking comprising datasets, clustering quality metrics, and standardized runtime, memory, and scalability evaluation. SCPP further provides extensive documentation, practical examples, automated testing, and seamless integration with the scientific Python ecosystem, enabling reproducible experimentation and straightforward extension with new algorithms. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/soft-clustering/soft-clustering.

AI.tldr: SCPP is an open-source Python framework that standardizes soft clustering algorithms with scikit-learn compatibility, integrating 40 algorithms and comprehensive benchmarking tools.
AI.motivation: There's a need to standardize and unify the diverse landscape of soft clustering methods (fuzzy, probabilistic, graph-based, etc.) which currently lack consistent interfaces, evaluation procedures, and reproducibility tools, making comparison and application difficult.
AI.method: SCPP creates a canonical scikit-learn-compatible estimator interface that standardizes training, prediction, membership representation, evaluation, and benchmarking. It integrates 40 representative algorithms and provides datasets, clustering quality metrics, runtime/memory/scalability evaluation, documentation, examples, testing, and ecosystem integration.
AI.result: The framework successfully establishes a unified platform for soft clustering that enables reproducible experimentation, standardized comparison across diverse algorithms, and straightforward extension with new methods. It provides comprehensive benchmarking capabilities and seamless integration with the Python scientific ecosystem.
AI.conclusion: SCPP addresses the fragmentation in soft clustering by providing a standardized, extensible framework that facilitates reproducible research, practical application, and algorithm development, advancing the field through improved accessibility and comparability of soft clustering methods.

title: EvoDRC: A Self-Evolving Agentic Framework for Automated DRC Violation Repair
summary: Design rule check (DRC) closure remains a major bottleneck in advanced-node physical design. Although detailed routers are rule-aware, residual design rule violations (DRVs) often require manual engineering change order iterations. Automating this process is challenging because repairs must account for complex geometric interactions, preserve circuit connectivity, and avoid introducing new violations. We present EvoDRC, a skill-evolution framework for agentic block-level DRC repair. EvoDRC initializes layer-specific repair skills using knowledge distilled from an unrelated reference design and continuously evolves these skills using traceable repair experience collected from the target design. EvoDRC decomposes the layout into bounded repair regions and assigns an LLM repair agent to each region. Local DRC analysis, connectivity-checking, and impact-preview tools provide feedback on proposed modifications. Repair operations and their resulting DRV changes are stored in a knowledge database and used to evolve the repair skills. Experiments on seven block-level designs from the DAC26 DRC Benchmark show that EvoDRC achieves a 73.5\% overall reduction compared to the reported baseline.

AI.tldr: EvoDRC is an AI-driven framework that uses skill evolution and LLM agents to automate DRC violation repair in advanced-node physical design, achieving 73.5% reduction in violations compared to baseline.
AI.motivation: DRC closure is a major bottleneck in advanced-node physical design, often requiring manual engineering change order iterations due to residual design rule violations that detailed routers cannot fully eliminate. The challenge lies in automating repairs while accounting for complex geometric interactions, preserving circuit connectivity, and avoiding new violations.
AI.method: EvoDRC uses a skill-evolution framework that: 1) initializes layer-specific repair skills using knowledge distilled from an unrelated reference design, 2) decomposes layouts into bounded repair regions, 3) assigns LLM repair agents to each region, 4) uses local DRC analysis, connectivity-checking, and impact-preview tools for feedback, and 5) evolves repair skills continuously using traceable repair experience stored in a knowledge database.
AI.result: Experiments on seven block-level designs from the DAC26 DRC Benchmark show that EvoDRC achieves a 73.5% overall reduction in DRC violations compared to the reported baseline.
AI.conclusion: EvoDRC demonstrates an effective AI-driven approach to automate DRC repair using skill evolution and LLM agents, significantly reducing the need for manual engineering iterations and addressing the major bottleneck in advanced-node physical design.

title: HypEMBER: Hypernetwork-based Ensemble for Robust Policy Learning of Parametrized Dynamical Systems
summary: In this work we investigate reinforcement learning (RL) as a framework for the robust control of parametrized dynamical systems in presence of measurements and model uncertainties. High-dimensional state spaces, expensive numerical solvers, the partial knowledge of the governing equations, and the dependence on physical parameters that may be uncertain or difficult to estimate accurately, make the use of standard RL approaches computationally unfeasible. Indeed, lack of robustness and poor generalization across parameter variations are further amplified in presence of noisy or incomplete measurements, ultimately hampering control performance. To address these challenges, we introduce HypEMBER, a novel RL framework based on the combination of hypernetworks and ensemble learning. In the proposed approach, both the policy and value functions are represented through hypernetworks that generate the weights of the underlying models conditioned on the physical parameters of the system, thereby enabling parametric generalization across different dynamical regimes. In addition, an ensemble of policy and value approximators is employed to quantify epistemic uncertainty, leading to improved exploration strategies and enhanced robustness during and after training. The performance of the proposed framework is assessed on two representative parametrized control problems: (i) the one-dimensional Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation and (ii) a particle-navigation task in a two-dimensional time-dependent gyre flow, focusing on robustness with respect to measurement noise and parameter misspecification. Numerical results demonstrate that HypEMBER consistently improves training stability and sample efficiency, while achieving superior robustness to uncertainties affecting both the system dynamics and the available observations, in comparison with state-of-the-art RL methods.

AI.tldr: HypEMBER is a new RL framework combining hypernetworks and ensemble learning for robust control of parametrized dynamical systems with uncertainties.
AI.motivation: Standard RL approaches struggle with high-dimensional state spaces, expensive solvers, partial knowledge of governing equations, parameter uncertainties, measurement noise, and poor generalization across parameter variations, making robust control computationally unfeasible.
AI.method: HypEMBER uses hypernetworks to generate policy and value function weights conditioned on physical parameters, enabling parametric generalization. An ensemble of approximators quantifies epistemic uncertainty for improved exploration and robustness.
AI.result: The framework was tested on 1D Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation and 2D particle-navigation tasks. It consistently improved training stability, sample efficiency, and showed superior robustness to uncertainties compared to state-of-the-art RL methods.
AI.conclusion: HypEMBER successfully addresses robustness challenges in parametrized dynamical system control by leveraging hypernetworks for parameter conditioning and ensemble methods for uncertainty quantification, demonstrating practical applicability to complex control problems.

title: Global Difference Constraint Propagation for Constraint Programming
summary: Difference constraints of the form $x - y \leq d$ are well studied, with efficient algorithms for satisfaction and implication, because of their connection to shortest paths. Finite domain propagation algorithms, however, typically do not make use of these algorithms, and treat each difference constraint as a separate propagator. Propagation does guarantee completeness of solving, but can be needlessly slow. In this paper we describe how to build a (bounds consistent) global propagator for difference constraints that treats them all simultaneously. SAT modulo theory solvers have included theory solvers for difference constraints for some time. While a theory solver for difference constraints gives the basis of a global difference constraint propagator, we show how the requirements on the propagator are quite different. Crucially, we show how to explain propagations by a global difference constraint propagator, in order to use it within a lazy clause generation solver. We give experiments showing that treating difference constraints globally can substantially improve on the standard propagation approach.

AI.tldr: A global propagator for difference constraints in finite domain constraint programming that treats all constraints simultaneously, enabling more efficient propagation than individual propagators.
AI.motivation: Finite domain propagation algorithms typically treat each difference constraint as a separate propagator, which is needlessly slow despite guaranteeing completeness. While SAT modulo theory solvers have included difference constraint theory solvers, there's a need for efficient global propagation in constraint programming settings.
AI.method: Develop a bounds consistent global propagator for difference constraints that treats all constraints simultaneously. The approach adapts theory solver concepts from SAT modulo theory to constraint programming requirements, with emphasis on generating explanations for propagations to integrate with lazy clause generation solvers.
AI.result: Experimental results show that treating difference constraints globally can substantially improve performance over the standard propagation approach using individual propagators.
AI.conclusion: A global difference constraint propagator, designed to work within lazy clause generation solvers with proper explanation capabilities, offers significant performance advantages over traditional per-constraint propagation methods in constraint programming.

title: Anatomy of a Sound Neural Reasoner: One-Shot Amortization, First-Pass Poisoning, and Search Inertness in Clue-Rich Completion
summary: Neural solvers are built to deduce, branch, and revise intermediate states. The Lattice Deduction Transformer (LDT) appears to do exactly that. In clue-rich Sudoku, it does not: one forward pass commits essentially the entire grid (every blank cell on standard 6x6, 94-96% on augmented 9x9), turning the iterative solver into a one-shot predictor wrapped in an exact verifier. All hard-slice failures are decided before search begins, when the first pass confidently deletes a value required by the true solution. We call this first-pass poisoning. Adding learned branching, MRV, backtracking, value exclusion, and shared nogoods (CoLT) does not change which Sudoku instances are solved; it cuts repeated invalid derivations 1,497-fold. At the frozen training budget, constraint-graph attention alone matches full-CoLT accuracy, while positional tables recover only under substantially longer training, indicating an optimization and sample-efficiency advantage rather than an absolute capacity difference. The diagnosis predicts two effective interventions. Digit-permutation augmentation raises 9x9 accuracy from below 1% to 96.5 +/- 0.3 across three training seeds on a symmetry-disjoint split. Test-time union over symmetry-transformed passes raises all three hard-slice checkpoints from 72.8-78.9% to 100% without retraining. On from-scratch graph coloring, one-shot behavior disappears and search changes accuracy. In clue-rich completion, LDT-like systems are one-shot amortized predictors rather than learned search procedures: accuracy is determined by calibration and symmetry, while search primarily removes computational waste.

AI.tldr: LDT-based neural solvers for clue-rich Sudoku behave as one-shot predictors rather than iterative solvers, with first-pass poisoning determining outcomes; search mainly reduces computational waste, while accuracy depends on calibration and symmetry.
AI.motivation: To understand why neural solvers like Lattice Deduction Transformer (LDT) fail on clue-rich Sudoku despite being designed for iterative deduction, and to analyze whether they function as learned search procedures or one-shot predictors.
AI.method: Analyze LDT's behavior on Sudoku: measure commitment in first forward pass, test interventions (learned branching, MRV, backtracking, value exclusion, shared nogoods/CoLT), compare constraint-graph attention vs positional tables, and evaluate symmetry-based interventions (digit-permutation augmentation and test-time union over symmetry-transformed passes).
AI.result: First pass commits most grid (100% on 6x6, 94-96% on 9x9), creating "first-pass poisoning" that determines outcomes. Search reduces invalid derivations 1,497x but doesn't change which instances are solved. Digit-permutation augmentation boosts 9x9 accuracy from <1% to 96.5%. Test-time symmetry union raises checkpoints from 72.8-78.9% to 100%. On graph coloring (not clue-rich), one-shot behavior disappears and search matters.
AI.conclusion: In clue-rich completion tasks like Sudoku, LDT-like systems function as one-shot amortized predictors, not learned search procedures. Accuracy is determined by calibration and symmetry properties, while search primarily eliminates computational waste rather than improving fundamental solving capability.

title: Reading and Steering Representations of Materials-Science Mechanisms in an Open-Weight Language Model
summary: Large language models can answer scientific questions, yet a correct output does not reveal whether the model represents or uses the governing physics. Here we show that materials science mechanism information in the open-weight google/gemma-4-E4B-it model has three experimentally separable forms: concepts are readable in individual hidden states, constitutive orientation is carried by controlled transformations between states, and selected internal representations causally control engineering answers. We combine matched direct and Jacobian vocabulary readouts, option-free state geometry, a 60-law counterfactual benchmark and causal interventions. In 50 held-out materials descriptions, three independently fitted Jacobian lenses reproduced concept ranks, and target-free word sets from both readouts enabled blinded identification of 9 of 10 mechanism families. A separate 72-prompt benchmark produced mechanism-specific hidden-state neighborhoods, but an exact graph audit showed that this apparent physical organization was equally explained by numerical comparison. We therefore compared otherwise identical prompts in which only the direction of the physical input was reversed, asking whether the resulting hidden-state movement followed the supplied constitutive law. These state transformations ordered direct, physically neutral and inverse laws across 60 frozen relations and correctly oriented 39 of 40 directional laws, whereas lexical controls were near chance. Bidirectional interventions shifted answer probabilities toward or away from the physically appropriate outcome across all 12 matched cases, while counterfactual state patches transferred opposing decision signals across mechanisms and answer formats. Physical relationships were therefore more visible in controlled state changes than in absolute states alone.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how physics knowledge is represented in LLMs, showing that materials science mechanisms exist in three forms: concepts in individual states, constitutive orientation in state transformations, and causal control of answers. The study uses multiple experimental techniques to reveal that physical relationships are more visible in controlled state changes than in absolute states alone.
AI.motivation: While large language models can answer scientific questions correctly, it's unclear whether they actually understand and represent the governing physics behind their answers. The researchers want to determine how materials science mechanism information is encoded and used in LLMs, specifically whether the models merely reproduce statistical patterns or genuinely represent physical concepts and relationships.
AI.method: The study uses multiple experimental approaches: 1) matched direct and Jacobian vocabulary readouts to analyze concept representation, 2) option-free state geometry analysis, 3) a 60-law counterfactual benchmark for testing physics understanding, and 4) causal interventions to test model behavior. They tested the Google/gemma-4-E4B-it model on materials science prompts, including 50 held-out descriptions and a separate 72-prompt benchmark, and conducted bidirectional interventions and counterfactual state patches.
AI.result: 1) Jacobian lenses reproduced concept ranks across 50 materials descriptions. 2) Target-free word sets enabled blinded identification of 9 out of 10 mechanism families. 3) Hidden-state neighborhoods were mechanism-specific but graph audit showed this could be explained by numerical comparison. 4) State transformations correctly ordered direct, neutral, and inverse laws across 60 relations and oriented 39 of 40 directional laws (vs. chance-level lexical controls). 5) Bidirectional interventions shifted answer probabilities appropriately across all 12 cases. 6) Counterfactual state patches transferred opposing decision signals across mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Physical relationships in LLMs are more visible in controlled state changes than in absolute states alone. The models represent materials science mechanisms in three separable forms: individual concepts in hidden states, constitutive orientation through state transformations, and causal control over engineering answers. This suggests LLMs do encode some level of physical understanding beyond mere pattern matching.

title: Expert-Guided Forecast Editing for Time-Series Foundation Models
summary: Time-series foundation models can forecast across heterogeneous domains without task-specific training, but their forecasts are fixed once produced and cannot directly incorporate task-specific expert feedback. We study expert-guided forecast editing: a frozen foundation model generates candidate future trajectories, and an expensive expert evaluator scores them to guide forecast revision. Under a tight query budget, two natural strategies sit at opposite ends: best-of-$N$ purely exploits the foundation model's predictive distribution, while optimization approaches mostly explore the forecast horizon as an unstructured high-dimensional vector. Each extreme is individually sub-optimal. We introduce \textbf{DEFT}, an expert-guided forecast editing framework that balances the two by first exploiting the foundation model's predictive samples in a decomposed trend--seasonal space, then exploring around them via component-wise refinement. DEFT queries the expert only on complete trajectories, then reuses scores for the trend and seasonal components that appeared in the queried recombinations. This lets each expert query provide structured component-level feedback while keeping the foundation model frozen. We compare DEFT against direct search approaches, including best-of-$N$, cross-entropy methods, and Bayesian optimization, under matched expert-query budgets. Across two forecasting benchmarks consisting of 78 datasets, three time-series foundation models, four feedback types, and seven query budgets, DEFT consistently improves the effectiveness of expert guidance. A molecular-dynamics case study further suggests that the same principle extends to more physically grounded feedback, supporting the hypothesis that sparse test-time guidance should be spent balancing prior exploitation with structured exploration.

AI.tldr: DEFT is a framework for expert-guided forecast editing that balances exploitation of foundation models' predictive distributions with structured exploration in trend-seasonal space, outperforming existing methods under tight query budgets.
AI.motivation: Time-series foundation models can forecast across domains but produce fixed forecasts that cannot incorporate expert feedback. Current approaches like best-of-N (purely exploit) or optimization methods (purely explore) are suboptimal under tight expert query budgets.
AI.method: DEFT first exploits foundation model's predictions by decomposing them into trend-seasonal components, then explores via component-wise refinement. It queries experts only on complete trajectories and reuses scores for components that appeared in queried recombinations, enabling component-level feedback while keeping foundation model frozen.
AI.result: Across 78 datasets, 3 time-series foundation models, 4 feedback types, and 7 query budgets, DEFT consistently improves expert guidance effectiveness compared to best-of-N, cross-entropy methods, and Bayesian optimization.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that sparse test-time guidance should balance prior exploitation with structured exploration, with applications extending to physically grounded domains like molecular dynamics.

title: PRO-LONG: Programmatic Memory Enables Long-Horizon Reasoning
summary: Long-horizon tasks require sustained perception, reasoning, and exploration, and are a persistent challenge for large language model (LLM) agents. This gap is reflected in their limited performance on continual learning benchmarks such as ARC-AGI-3, especially when models are evaluated out of the box. Various agent harnesses have been proposed to close this gap, and each commits to a strategy for handling long sequences of observations, i.e., what information to save from the environment and how to load it into model context, a choice we argue is particularly consequential. Existing methods for context management face a significant tradeoff, as preserving more information makes retrieving relevant details less tractable. We propose PRO-LONG, a minimal context management framework built around programmatic memory for LLM agents in long-horizon, exploratory settings. PRO-LONG addresses the tradeoff by keeping a complete, structured interaction log and capitalizing on recent progress in coding agents to search this history efficiently. On the full ARC-AGI-3 public game set, PRO-LONG improves over a base coding agent by an average of 18.0 percentage points across frontier models, and matches or exceeds state-of-the-art specialized harnesses (up to 76.1% pass@1) while using 4.2-5.8x fewer tokens. With Fable 5, PRO-LONG achieves 97.4% best@2 at a total cost of \$1,750. Relevant code and logs are available at https://github.com/alexisfox7/PRO-LONG.

AI.tldr: PRO-LONG is a programmatic memory framework that improves LLM agent performance on long-horizon tasks by efficiently managing context through structured interaction logs and coding-based search, achieving state-of-the-art results on ARC-AGI-3 with significantly reduced token usage.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring sustained perception, reasoning, and exploration, as evidenced by limited performance on continual learning benchmarks like ARC-AGI-3. Existing context management methods face a fundamental tradeoff: preserving more information makes retrieving relevant details less tractable.
AI.method: PRO-LONG is a minimal context management framework built around programmatic memory. It keeps a complete, structured interaction log and leverages recent progress in coding agents to efficiently search through this history. This approach maintains comprehensive records while enabling effective information retrieval.
AI.result: On the full ARC-AGI-3 public game set, PRO-LONG improves over a base coding agent by an average of 18.0 percentage points across frontier models. It matches or exceeds state-of-the-art specialized harnesses (up to 76.1% pass@1) while using 4.2-5.8x fewer tokens. With Fable 5, PRO-LONG achieves 97.4% best@2 at a total cost of $1,750.
AI.conclusion: PRO-LONG successfully addresses the context management tradeoff in long-horizon LLM agent tasks by combining structured interaction logging with coding-based search, enabling both comprehensive information preservation and efficient retrieval while significantly reducing computational costs.

title: Efficient Clustering with Provable Guardrails for LLM Inference at Scale
comment: Accepted for presentation at ICML HiLD workshop 2026 (non-archival)
summary: Scaling LLM-based applications to millions of users is bottlenecked by the inference cost and latency of modern foundation models. A natural fix is to cluster the inputs and call the LLM only on cluster representatives, letting other members inherit the output -- but this is only safe if each member is measurably close to its representative. Existing clustering methods do not offer such per-sample quality control at scale: none jointly guarantee a minimal within-cluster similarity, exact matching of categorical attributes, and scalability to tens of millions of samples. We propose a two-stage algorithm that generates initial clusters with Mini-batch K-Means, then greedily selects representatives within each initial cluster -- a step equivalent to the Johnson-Chvatal heuristic for Set Cover over alpha-balls in embedding space. The algorithm enforces the similarity and attribute guardrails exactly by construction, and runs in $O(nd + n^2 d/K)$ time and $O(nd + n^2/K^2)$ memory for $n$ samples, feature dimension $d$, and $K$ initial clusters -- linear in $n$ when $K$ grows proportionally with $n$. We provide benchmarks against common clustering methods on internal and public datasets: our method not only delivers per-sample guardrails but also runs 10-1000x faster and scales to data sizes where most standard methods become intractable. Deployed on 38 million customers for a persona-based recommender, the clustering method cut downstream cost and latency by 50-fold while preserving personalization and unblocked the production launch.

AI.tldr: A two-stage clustering algorithm that enables LLM applications to scale to millions of users by clustering inputs and calling LLMs only on cluster representatives, with guaranteed per-sample similarity constraints and exact attribute matching.
AI.motivation: Scaling LLM-based applications to millions of users is bottlenecked by high inference costs and latency of modern foundation models. Current clustering methods lack per-sample quality control at scale, cannot guarantee minimal within-cluster similarity and exact attribute matching simultaneously, and don't scale to tens of millions of samples.
AI.method: Two-stage algorithm: 1) Generate initial clusters using Mini-batch K-Means, 2) Greedily select representatives within each initial cluster (equivalent to Johnson-Chvatal heuristic for Set Cover over alpha-balls in embedding space). The method enforces similarity and attribute guardrails exactly by construction, with time complexity O(nd + n²d/K) and memory O(nd + n²/K²) for n samples, dimension d, and K initial clusters.
AI.result: Method delivers per-sample guardrails while running 10-1000x faster than common clustering methods, scaling to data sizes where standard methods become intractable. Deployed on 38 million customers for a persona-based recommender, it cut downstream cost and latency by 50-fold while preserving personalization and enabling production launch.
AI.conclusion: The proposed clustering algorithm provides scalable, guaranteed-quality clustering for LLM applications, enabling significant cost and latency reductions while maintaining personalization at scale, solving key bottlenecks in deploying LLMs to millions of users.

title: TRUST-ESD: A Risk-Calibrated and Governance-Aware AI Framework for Enterprise Strategic Decision Support Under Uncertainty
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables. Submitted to APWeb-WAIM 2026, Danang, Vietnam, September 7-9, 2026
summary: Enterprise strategic decision support requires AI systems that are not only accurate, but also uncertainty-aware, risk-calibrated, explainable, and governance-compliant. This paper proposes TRUST-ESD, a risk-calibrated and governance-aware framework for enterprise decision support under uncertainty. TRUST-ESD evaluates feasible counterfactual strategies through predictive utility estimation, conformal uncertainty calibration, CVaR-based downside-risk scoring, risk-memory retrieval, policy-as-code governance, explainability, and human oversight. Unlike prediction-only methods that select actions by maximum expected utility, TRUST-ESD recommends strategies that balance value, reliability, risk exposure, and compliance. Experimental results show that TRUST-ESD improves risk-adjusted utility by 7.95%, reduces risk exposure by 23.22%, reduces CVaR by 23.78%, lowers calibration error by 13.89%, improves explanation fidelity by 10.90%, and increases governance compliance by 9.76% compared with strong uncertainty-aware baselines, while maintaining competitive predictive accuracy. Ablation and case-study analyses further confirm that uncertainty calibration, downside-risk scoring, risk memory, explainability, and governance validation jointly improve trustworthy enterprise decision-making.

AI.tldr: TRUST-ESD is a risk-calibrated, governance-aware AI framework for enterprise decision support that balances value, reliability, risk exposure, and compliance through uncertainty calibration, CVaR-based risk scoring, and policy-as-code governance.
AI.motivation: Current enterprise AI systems need to go beyond mere accuracy to be uncertainty-aware, risk-calibrated, explainable, and governance-compliant for strategic decision support. Prediction-only methods that select actions by maximum expected utility are insufficient because they don't account for risk exposure, compliance requirements, and reliability concerns in real-world enterprise settings.
AI.method: TRUST-ESD evaluates counterfactual strategies through: 1) predictive utility estimation, 2) conformal uncertainty calibration, 3) CVaR-based downside-risk scoring, 4) risk-memory retrieval from past decisions, 5) policy-as-code governance validation, 6) explainability mechanisms, and 7) human oversight integration. The framework recommends strategies balancing multiple trustworthiness dimensions.
AI.result: TRUST-ESD improves risk-adjusted utility by 7.95%, reduces risk exposure by 23.22%, reduces CVaR by 23.78%, lowers calibration error by 13.89%, improves explanation fidelity by 10.90%, and increases governance compliance by 9.76% compared with strong uncertainty-aware baselines, while maintaining competitive predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty calibration, downside-risk scoring, risk memory, explainability, and governance validation jointly improve trustworthy enterprise decision-making. TRUST-ESD provides a comprehensive framework that addresses the multi-faceted requirements of enterprise strategic decision support beyond traditional prediction-only approaches.

authors: Venkata Naga Sai Vishnu Rohit Pulipaka
title: How Fast Can Reward Models Score? A Systems Study of C++ and PyTorch Inference Runtimes for RLHF
summary: In RLHF pipelines, reward scoring blocks policy updates. Slow scoring bottlenecks the entire loop, since no update runs until every rollout gets a score. And yet most setups just default to PyTorch eager mode or torch.compile, no one checks if that's actually fastest. Scoring itself is small. Rollout generation eats far more of a typical RLHF step. But scoring and generation fight over the same CPU and GPU resources, so a faster scoring engine doesn't shrink step time on its own. It mainly frees up capacity generation can use instead. We built a native C++ inference engine on ONNX Runtime. First step: confirm correctness. Output matched the PyTorch reference to 5.7 x 10^-6 on CPU and 4.2 x 10^-3 on GPU, close enough to trust. Then we tested it against PyTorch eager mode, torch.compile, and FastAPI, on both CPU and GPU. CPU was decisive. Our engine beat every baseline, confidence intervals didn't even overlap. GPU gave a different view: we beat PyTorch and FastAPI, but torch.compile came out ahead. Further testing traced the speedup to ONNX Runtime itself, not C++ as a language. And batching strategy mattered more than either the language or the runtime choice, more than we expected. The results are from repeated, independent runs, since single runs just aren't reliable enough to trust.

AI.tldr: Building a native C++ inference engine on ONNX Runtime for faster reward scoring in RLHF pipelines, which significantly outperforms PyTorch eager mode and FastAPI on CPU, but torch.compile still wins on GPU.
AI.motivation: Reward scoring creates a bottleneck in RLHF pipelines because it blocks policy updates until every rollout gets a score. Most setups default to PyTorch eager mode or torch.compile without verifying if these are actually the fastest options. Scoring itself is computationally small compared to rollout generation, but both processes compete for the same CPU/GPU resources.
AI.method: Developed a native C++ inference engine on ONNX Runtime. First verified correctness by comparing outputs with PyTorch reference (5.7×10^-6 error on CPU, 4.2×10^-3 on GPU). Then benchmarked against PyTorch eager mode, torch.compile, and FastAPI on both CPU and GPU. Used repeated, independent runs for reliability rather than single runs.
AI.result: On CPU: The C++ engine decisively beat all baselines with non-overlapping confidence intervals. On GPU: The engine beat PyTorch and FastAPI, but torch.compile performed better. Further analysis showed the speedup came from ONNX Runtime itself rather than C++ as a language, and batching strategy had more impact than language or runtime choice.
AI.conclusion: A native C++ inference engine on ONNX Runtime provides significant speed improvements for reward scoring in RLHF pipelines, particularly on CPU, but the choice of batching strategy matters more than language or runtime selection. GPU performance requires careful consideration as torch.compile can outperform custom implementations.

title: CUSUM-Shaped Inference-Time Monitoring and Targeted Re-Decoding for Quantized Small Language Model Reasoning
summary: Quantized small autoregressive reasoning models can enter long, repetitive, or unproductive trajectories, yet inference-time compute is usually allocated without observing how a trajectory develops. Building on an earlier token-level e-CUSUM controller, we develop MGT-B (Monitoring-Guided Test-time Backtracking), a revised external controller that maps overlapping windows of pre-sampling uncertainty and degeneration features to position-conditional empirical tail probabilities, accumulates mixture betting factors with a CUSUM-shaped reset, and responds to an alarm by estimating a rollback point, restoring token and key-value-cache state, and performing constrained re-decoding. To audit whether the effect persists on problem identities first observed after the manual choice of log threshold h = 10, we retrospectively exclude 260 IDs present in pre-threshold artifacts and retain the chronologically first post-threshold pair for each remaining ID, yielding a 240-pair chronology-audit set. On this set, accuracy changes from 82/240 to 88/240 (+2.50 percentage points; 13 corrections, 7 regressions; exact McNemar p = 0.2632; paired bootstrap 95% interval [-1.25, +6.25]). A broader 467-pair historical-coverage set of seed-matched pairs changes accuracy from 146/467 to 167/467 (+4.50 points; McNemar p = 0.000753), but includes 200 seed-1 IDs available before or during threshold selection and is reported only as an exploratory estimate. All 316 no-alarm outputs in the 467-pair set are identical to vanilla, while the 151 alarmed trajectories contain 29 corrections and 8 regressions. Neither analysis is confirmatory, and the empirical factors are not established as a valid e-process or e-detector. The results support a selective monitoring-and-repair mechanism for the studied MATH-500 setting, rather than a general or theoretically certified reasoning improvement.

AI.tldr: A monitoring-guided backtracking method for quantized autoregressive models that detects and corrects unproductive reasoning trajectories at inference time.
AI.motivation: Quantized small autoregressive reasoning models can enter long, repetitive, or unproductive reasoning trajectories during inference, but current methods allocate compute without observing how trajectories develop, leading to inefficient or incorrect outputs.
AI.method: Developed MGT-B (Monitoring-Guided Test-time Backtracking), an external controller that monitors overlapping windows of pre-sampling uncertainty and degeneration features, maps them to empirical tail probabilities, accumulates mixture betting factors with a CUSUM-shaped reset mechanism, and responds to alarms by estimating rollback points, restoring token and key-value-cache state, and performing constrained re-decoding.
AI.result: On a 240-pair chronology-audit set, accuracy improved from 82/240 to 88/240 (+2.50 percentage points). On a larger 467-pair historical-coverage set, accuracy improved from 146/467 to 167/467 (+4.50 points). The method corrected 29 trajectories but caused 8 regressions in alarmed cases, while all 316 no-alarm outputs remained identical to vanilla inference.
AI.conclusion: The results support a selective monitoring-and-repair mechanism for the studied MATH-500 setting, but this is not a general or theoretically certified reasoning improvement. The empirical factors are not established as valid e-processes or e-detectors, and the analyses are exploratory rather than confirmatory.

title: Koopman Dreamer: Spectrally Constrained Latent Dynamics for Stable World-Model Imagination
summary: Latent world models improve sample efficiency in continuous control by optimizing policies over imagined latent trajectories, but common neural transitions offer limited direct control over modal persistence and error accumulation in long rollouts. We propose Koopman Dreamer, a Dreamer-style world model with a spectrally constrained deterministic latent dynamics core. Its Koopman-inspired backbone uses two-dimensional rotation--scaling blocks with bounded radii to represent damping, rotation, and near-periodic modes. Linear and low-rank bilinear action terms capture global and state-dependent control effects, while stochastic-state modulation supplies local correction information. To reduce the mismatch between posterior-conditioned training and prior-only imagination, the model combines posterior-conditioned EMA teacher targets with one-step consistency, multi-step rollout, and open-loop observation-prediction objectives. We further derive a multi-step rollout-error bound that separates amplification by the spectral backbone and bilinear interaction from the additive effects of stochastic-state mismatch and modeling residuals, clarifying the trade-off between error attenuation and long-term information retention. Experimental results on proprioceptive continuous-control tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite and UAV-LiDAR autonomous navigation demonstrate that Koopman Dreamer improves the stability of long-horizon latent rollouts and achieves stronger closed-loop control performance on tasks that rely on high-quality multi-step imagination.

AI.tldr: Koopman Dreamer improves long-horizon imagination in latent world models using spectrally constrained Koopman-inspired dynamics with rotation-scaling blocks and bilinear action terms, achieving better stability and control performance in continuous control tasks.
AI.motivation: Latent world models have limited control over modal persistence and error accumulation during long rollouts, which hampers sample efficiency in continuous control tasks that rely on high-quality multi-step imagination.
AI.method: Proposes Koopman Dreamer with spectrally constrained deterministic latent dynamics using 2D rotation-scaling blocks for damping/rotation/near-periodic modes, linear+bilinear action terms, and stochastic-state modulation. Combines posterior-conditioned EMA teacher targets with one-step consistency, multi-step rollout, and open-loop prediction objectives to reduce training-imagination mismatch.
AI.result: Experimental results on DeepMind Control Suite and UAV-LiDAR navigation show improved stability of long-horizon latent rollouts and stronger closed-loop control performance on tasks requiring high-quality multi-step imagination.
AI.conclusion: Koopman Dreamer's spectrally constrained dynamics backbone with Koopman-inspired structure enables better error control and information retention in long rollouts, leading to improved sample efficiency and control performance in continuous control tasks.

title: PoTRE: Test-Time Reasoning inspired by Cognitive Heterogeneity
comment: Accepted at Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR 2026)
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at many tasks, they frequently struggle with complex reasoning that requires long-horizon planning and iterative error correction. Furthermore, standard single-stream prompting proves brittle when models encounter novel abstractions or rigorous domain constraints. We introduce PoTRE (Poly-Topological Reasoning Ensembles), a heterogeneous framework that decouples inference into four agents: (1) Adversarial Refinement Agent, (2) Hierarchical strategic Planning Agent, (3) Spectrum Search Agent, and (4) Direct Chain Agent. A final Task-Adaptive Aggregation Layer dynamically reconciles these perspectives -- via final candidate selection, semantic synthesis, or neuro-symbolic verification -- to produce a robust global solution. We evaluate PoTRE on three frontier benchmarks: ARC-AGI-2, Humanity's Last Exam (HLE), and PRBench Finance. PoTRE achieves state-of-the-art accuracy of 49.92% on HLE, surpassing the previous best official score. We demonstrate that this architectural heterogeneity achieves improved reasoning performance using similar or fewer inference tokens compared to heavily scaled homogeneous baselines.

AI.tldr: PoTRE (Poly-Topological Reasoning Ensembles) is a heterogeneous framework using four specialized agents for complex reasoning tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results on frontier benchmarks while using fewer tokens than homogeneous baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with complex reasoning requiring long-horizon planning and iterative error correction, especially when encountering novel abstractions or domain constraints. Standard single-stream prompting is brittle for such tasks.
AI.method: Introduces PoTRE, a framework with four specialized agents: (1) Adversarial Refinement Agent, (2) Hierarchical strategic Planning Agent, (3) Spectrum Search Agent, and (4) Direct Chain Agent. Uses a Task-Adaptive Aggregation Layer that dynamically reconciles these perspectives through candidate selection, semantic synthesis, or neuro-symbolic verification.
